title: Another ALICE
author: Shawn Jordan

chapters.title: THE FIRST ENTRY
paragraphs: Adam’s ship tipped over the horizon, and the whole universe turned like the hands on a clock, quickening, racing around in a monumental vortex of light and dark and all things between and beyond. The appalling silence of deep space crescendoed recklessly to a roar, then a scream, then an awesome symphony of sound and static.

The ship groaned, straining under the pressure of gravity beyond measure, or perhaps it was him making these mournful cries that resonated in the world like the ring of a bell. Where it came from, it did not matter – not anymore – because for a moment, it was all the same stuff: the man and his vessel.

Adam could no longer tell if his eyes were open or closed. The ship’s interior was awash in darkness, and yet he felt his pupils tightening and his retinas burning as if we were staring brazenly into the core of a magnanimous sun. An omnipresent hum vibrated through him, a sustained Ohm that seemed to originate somehow from within and without. His entire body felt like it was covered in a swarm of bees, their wings beating relentlessly at the air. They thrummed in a resounding drone that enveloped him, then entered him. The cacophony rattled down in the most cavernous depths of his viscera, as the meticulous workings of the hive dissected him into pieces from within. This was what he had feared most – that crossing this threshold might tear him and everything apart – an expedition ended upon entry, to fail before even having a chance.

Adam opened his mouth, expecting perhaps to say a dying word, or at least to release the thriving hive that pulsed through every capillary of his being. He gulped at the air, fighting the feeling that clenched at his throat. He gasped an insistent breath and readied himself for his final cry, a death knell to bookend the infantile wails of his own birth.
Yet this was not the end of his story.

The world quite suddenly snapped into stillness. The bees vanished, and Adam felt his body again in the darkness. He wriggled his fingers, checking that he felt each in turn. His belly pressed with each breath against the interior of his suit. He could feel the straps again, holding him in place, though they felt tightened by the passage, practically suffocating him. He hastily unfastened them, taking a beat to relish in the fullness of his refound breath.

Around him the ship began to reawaken. Adam heard first the hum of a fan, then spotted the flicker of lights like pinprick stars on the control panel. “ALICE, are you still with me?” His words felt suspended in the air, unable to traverse the cockpit to the microphone on the panel. He sat in the stillness, contemplating with growing fear his solitude. There was a cough of static, but in it he could make out the words:
I AM

A soft smile came over his face. For the moment, he wasn’t alone. “That was some ride, huh?” he asked, still feeling the trembling of the psychedelic hive that had found them in the darkness. “Go ahead and run diagnostics. No rush – I’m guessing you’re still coming back online.” The lights on the panel proliferated and blinked in ripples that echoed out from the control panel as Adam found the strength to rise to his feet again. He felt limber, as though the jostling of the ship had knocked loose long-held knots in his bones.

He made his way to the center of the circular cockpit, where a large multi-paneled window allowed him to see what waited beneath the ship. Looking through it though, he saw only static. His brow furrowed. The panels beneath his feet were simply screens – illuminated projections from cameras embedded along the ship’s curved hull. No window made to date was known to be able to withstand the immense gravitational forces of a black hole. The static on the screens seemed slower than he expected, a sign that the ship’s generators were struggling to keep up with the energetic demands of bringing sight to its captain.

Adam squinted down at the muted gray puddle that laboriously pulsed and bristled. It made him uneasy, being blind as he was here. What lay beyond the ship’s bounds was still a mystery – at best there were reasonable predictions of what could be found within the mouths of these cosmic beasts. Some camps held fast to the idea that the holes were in fact tunnels – labyrinthian canals that connected the universe to other, more faraway universes. Adam had never been able to see the point of them this way. To him, the universe was already vast beyond reason, so what was the point of another one?

Others fell in with a more pointillist way of thinking. To enter the black hole was to be blown to smithereens, to give over to the ultimate consumption, to defrag down to the very essence of all matter. How this ship had managed to make the journey was beyond Adam. All he knew at this point was that he was still alive. “ALICE, any chance you could expedite repairs on the ship’s eyes? I would love to see what we’re working with here.” There was a beat of processing before ALICE responded:
THE SHIP’S VISION CAPACITIES ARE WORKING FINE, ADAM

Adam’s brow furrowed more deeply. He was struggling to make sense of what he was seeing. What had appeared to be static now seemed more like a fog, opaque and thick but moving just perceptibly in eddies that danced across the ship’s face.

“ALICE, show me the 360.” Immediately the cockpit came fully to life. The glow from the circular panel on the floor grew and spread out, away from Adam and covering the whole floor, then the rounded walls, then the ceiling, all gray except for the open porthole at the room’s apex. The mouth of the porthole was dark, the terminus of a vertical shaft that stretched upward to the opposite end of the round ship.

Adam’s eyes focused on the muted world around him, searching for anything, anyone that might be out there beyond the blanket of fog. Slowly, his eyes began to rest on a shape in the distance, a period floating on a blank page. It was another ship like his own. He truly wasn’t alone after all. Adam breathed in authentic relief at last. The threat of going at this expedition with only ALICE was beyond daunting. There was only so much they could do, limited as they were to hard parts and endless strings of bits.

Moreover, he knew his own limitations. His specialty was in studying the tiny gears that turned the machines of life, and in tinkering with those systems to call life forth where it was absent – a valuable skill for a terraforming expedition but insufficient alone. He needed his peers on this journey because they completed the necessary skill set.

They had once been a ragtag group of near-mad outcasts adrift on the burnout remnants of the galaxy. But with time they had grown into a modest movement – a herd of others, each bringing whatever gifts they could offer on the expedition. They were varied – physicists and nutritionists, orators and opera singers, fools and kings – and yet in more ways they were the same – hopeful dreamers with a tinge of reckless defiance of their own lives and a crippling reliance on life itself. All of them had been caught, trapped in a forgotten snare, a galaxy that humanity had ruined. They were among the remnants who hopped across depleted exoplanets, once viable and now empty scars, reminders of the wounds left by the primal desire to decimate one’s opponents until nothing remains but ash hanging in dead air.

This group had together developed a fascination with the black hole that sat resolutely at the galaxy’s center. Eons of astronomical exploration had beaten down the idea that humans might cross the dark divide between their galaxy and the billions of others beyond. The void that enshrouded their galaxy was too vast, somehow limitless though they could measure its bounds and see other galaxies beyond. Yet the human mind could not comprehend the scale of these journeys, and those that had sought to embark had vanished, dying quietly, or in few cases, had returned with minds battered beyond recognition.

In this time of hopeless wandering, the growing band of explorers had looked to invention for the sake of escape. To them, the answer was increasingly clear – if they couldn’t go outward to reach faraway worlds, perhaps they could instead escape inward. And yet the mysteries within the black hole at their galaxy’s center were even more enigmatic than the great beyond. To date, nothing known to man had left the endless pull of the gargantuan vortex. The only signal ever detected was the glow of barely existent radiation, the final mark of the unfortunate particles that skirted the hole’s rim in just the wrong way, ripping into two parts existing forever on opposite sides of the same coin, a duality destined to be unresolved for eternity.

Still, the rudimentary mathematics confidently supported the notion that there was a way through, and if there was a passage, then certainly there must be a destination. What that endpoint might be was the grand question, and here they were at the end of the line, the punctuation of a cosmic journey.

Adam’s eyes loosened their grip on the punctate shape beyond the walls of his ship. As his gaze continued to scan the static soup it landed on something in the distance above the other round ship. The period was instead an exclamation point, but the vertical column hanging above was unfamiliar. Their ships were all perfectly round – they had to be to make the journey, according to the mathematics. Any deviation from this perfect shape could be catastrophic, it had been warned. The explorers had spent many hours working the ships’ hulls, smoothing every imperfection in preparation for the entry, preparing for problems with low potential and catastrophic effects. Adam felt his stomach turn as he realized what he was seeing – a ship that had failed to remain intact through the entry.

He immediately wondered whether that ship had held a physicist or fool. Who had been the price of some imperceptible imperfection on their ship’s surface? Adam envisioned for a moment the remains of a particularly foul member of their crew – a corrupt ex-banker whose breath stank of rotting eggs and whose words felt as empty as the space between worlds. The thought of the ravenous beehive succeeding in its mission of dismemberment sent shivers through his insides. Whatever currency those forces dealt in were beyond the whims of man, and whoever had been in that vessel was now most certainly stretched brutally long like taffy in an apathetic pulling machine. Yet the pull was not met with a turn or a fold in the disfigured material. Instead the column of pieces stood steadfast there above the other intact ship, so perfectly round and safe.

Adam’s eye returned to the point, a dark speck in a broad sea, a companion adrift in another lifeboat just like him. Adam could feel his eyes growing tired as the point began to drift in his vision. He blinked hard, trying to refocus his gaze on the floating dot, and realized that it was, in fact, moving. And not just moving, but falling. Even from this far, he could see the characteristic acceleration of an object plucked by the gravitational pull of something, but what? What mighty beasts could possibly lie in the belly of the black hole with its massive, crushing power?

In a breath, the point was gone again, his companion lost to the monster in the mist. Adam stood in shock, not knowing what to do or say. For the first time, the true gravity of his situation sunk in. He felt impossibly small, helpless in a vessel he half-understood, looking out over – what? Was this swirling dust the beginnings of a star? Or was this just another world drowning in ash?

Adam could feel his heart starting to race as his mind explored every uncertainty, seeking something, anything to hold onto. His breathing was light – he had been holding his breath, he realized – and dots and flashes began to peek into the space around him. He felt his body about to fall and caught himself with a step forward. He trained his eyes ahead, breathing with intention to slow his racing mind and heart and any other parts in him that had seen fit to break into sprinting in that moment. The world settled back into focus and the twinkling lights in his eyes faded, but the dark spots stayed.

Adam rubbed at his eyes in disbelief, as the dancing gray fog shifted, revealing a dramatic constellation of ships and debris. All around, Adam could make them out – smatterings of black ink held fast in the sky, jagged streaks and tears punctuated by circular droplets.

Tears welled in Adam’s eyes, blurring the edges of the inkblot, making them run across the page. He felt overwhelmed by emotion – seeing more ships that had entered intact gave him immense, unexpected hope – and yet the harsh still life of the unfortunate dead hung mournfully over, a sick shadow monument of the cost paid to make this journey.

He realized how fortunate he had been to survive. By his count, there were far more ships lost than not, their repeating columns like the stems of headless notes, bars marking measures on unseen staffs, together carrying quiet melodies away and out of sight. It was a sad song that Adam read on the sky, notable for its gratuitous use of rests stretched long, gracious silence between each lonely black tone. He wondered whether his companions were singing or sobbing. He wasn’t even sure which he wanted to do himself. Then it occurred to him to ask. “ALICE, is there any chatter on the line?” ALICE took a beat to listen.
IT’S ALL SILENCE, ADAM.

He found himself picturing the worst – his peers eviscerated within the bowels of their perfectly whole ships. Perhaps the beehive had found a way to perform their surgery despite the travelers’ precautions. A dreadful hum filled his ears and for a moment Adam feared that one of the bees might still be at work somewhere in his mind. He shook his head urgently before realizing that the hum of the fan in the control panel had kicked on again. ALICE was busy at work and apparently feeling hot from all the processing. He was considering giving the ship a moment to refind equilibrium when he heard it – a flicker of sound barely perceptible over the fan’s whirring. “ALICE, listen! Focus on that sound.” In an instant the whole room went dark, every screen turning off at once.

The darkness was absolute. ALICE even stopped the whirring fan, bringing on a stark silence that left Adam feeling remarkably vulnerable. He kept his eyes open in spite of the blindness, waiting with ready ears in stillness for the signal to manifest once more. A single point of light illuminated on the panel across the room. ALICE had detected something again. A fractured message began to spill from the speakers in spits and sputters:
- go -      - he -      - key -

The voice was familiar, he could tell that much, but who they were and what they were trying to communicate was unclear. There was something resonant in the nuance of even these most fractured words, an element not yet mastered by ALICE or any other advanced language programs like them. Humans were never quite willing to allow their machines to fully replicate their dialects, and so their artificial voices were always just recognizably different. A trained ear could pick up on the constant reuse of the same sounds by the machine to build its words. What came out in the form of fashioned responses to human command always had a feeling of being piecemeal, a collection of parts more than a collective whole. To Adam, the mysterious workings of ALICE and other machines like them evoked images of Dr Frankenstein, sewing together a monster and zapping it with enough energy to awaken something like life in it. As a biologist, he found it admirable, astonishing even, seeing how this mimicry of life was so easily borne and set loose into the world. Despite their purported reservations, humans had still allowed their creations to infiltrate their worlds. Indeed, the explorers would not have made it even this far without the support of artificial assistance.
- keep -      - oh! -      - he -

The voice was most certainly human, more recognizable now. In his mind, Adam conjured the speaker: the king, perhaps. Or the astrophysicist. They who had projected sureness among the cadre of visionaries, though Adam now suspected that their theories and orations might have lacked any true cornerstone. How could they when their foundation - the black hole - was darkness made manifest?
“ keep going! ”
The message pressed through, at last fully formed.

Adam’s eyes welled with tears. It was a message of hope, of urgency. He could feel the speaker’s throat tightening with adrenaline. And here he stood, receiving the message loud and clear. “Keep going? ALICE, how do we do that?”
The fan came back to life, filling the room with a pervasive purr. The panels brightened slowly to a pale gray that enveloped the whole room.
I BELIEVE THAT I CAN MANIPULATE THE SHIP’S ENERGY FIELD TO PROPEL US FORWARD

Adam was impressed. He had known that ALICE would be strong in their analytical capacities, but he had not anticipated this degree of dynamism with the ship itself. To his knowledge this was not a function of the ship’s design, but who was he to question a mind far more capable than he in this regard? What was he going to do - step out and push the ship himself? “Where will we even go?”

The room fell silent again as the fan stopped for a beat. Adam’s eyes scanned the sky for any sign of movement among other intact pods. No matter how hard he urged another to drop from its suspended place, not one budged. He almost felt the need to repeat himself, but ALICE spoke up before he could:
I CAN’T PREDICT WHAT WE WILL FIND IF WE GO ON.
The frankness surprised Adam.
I CAN HELP US MOVE FORWARD. THE DECISION TO GO IS YOURS.
“ keep going ”

Adam’s mind returned to the image of the first ship dropping into the mist. Was this message from its pilot? Had they fallen into paradise beyond the passage? Adam could see a lush world hiding beneath the fog, a hidden cache of bountiful life unmarred by man. A sea of green trees, vines curling long and round, animals grazing lazily under sun and clouds. Was this the beckoning call of a friend waiting on the porch of a farmhouse, calling him home from an abundant field? Perhaps he couldn’t see his way for the harvest.
“ keep going ”

Or maybe this was the voice of another pilot in a suspended pod like his own, staring out at the same bleak sky from their own gray cocoon, urging someone else - anyone else - to make the next move, to take one for the team, make the leap of faith into the unknown oblivion and whatever waited beyond it. Oh, and then report back.
“ keep going ”

Adam’s gaze returned to the ghastly scene painted across the sky. The far-off blurs of parts and pieces reminded him of the peril of the moment. He wondered whether this was a delayed message from one of these ship’s pilots, a final cry before the hive tore them apart, a hope in their final breath that some other would carry on in their stead, carrying with them, what exactly? Their dreams? Their gear? Their names?
Adam returned to himself. Why was he here? “For life,” he whispered, out loud, as a reminder.

Adam had been diligent about uploading a wealth of genetic data into the chalice prior to departure. He had plans to reengineer familiar life in the world beyond the event horizon - hoping of course that there was a world to find. He had no expectations that he would find anything like the blue and green worlds of yesteryear past the black mask, but he felt certain that he could contribute to the terraformation that would be necessary, especially with the computational power of ALICE - his coy name for the chalice, belying the neutral timbre of their tone. With ALICE’s help, Adam knew that he could persevere as an attendant, a nurse, a greenhouse keeper for the seeds of life.

With clarity in his step, Adam returned to the seat and rebound himself with the straps. “For life,” he repeated, louder this time, to be sure ALICE heard him. The fan whirred with resolve in the control panel. Adam could feel that ALICE was ready. The ship’s interior felt like static getting ready to pop on his skin. His hairs stood at attention as his arms prickled with goosebumps.
ADAM, UNTIL WE GO THROUGH, WE ARE BLIND.
Adam’s eyes widened, hearing the uncertainty in ALICE’s words. This was it - the great flipping of the coin. Live or die. Go or stop. Together or alone.
WE GO TOGETHER, ADAM. ARE YOU READY?
Adam’s stomach dropped, anticipating the descent waiting just ahead. He detested the feeling of freefall. “For life.”
FOR LIFE.

The purr of the fan zipped into a fierce buzz that filled the ship’s interior suddenly. There were streamers and pops of static, light cascading in rivulets across the air in arcs that defied gravity, calligraphy writ in blinding color curly queuing in cursive nonsense all about the cockpit. Adam tasted sour and slop and sweet on his tongue, making it tingle frantically against his teeth. The gray on the screens went dark, only for a moment, then hissed white, so bright that Adam had to shut his eyes. Then the drop came.
Adam’s stomach lurched up into his throat, and his arms flew up to meet his face. He opened his eyes again. All around the room was abuzz and aglow, flickering somewhere between on and off.
“ keep going ”

Somehow over the cacophony of brilliant electric light and noise, Adam heard the message again, loud and clear. “I am!” he replied, fighting to hear himself over the dazzling storm. “I AM!” he repeated, needing to be heard in return. “I AM!”

chapters.title: IN THE GARDENS
paragraphs: Adam sat alone at his table, barely able to stomach the meal before him, a healthy serving of grayed greens steeping in a nutrient broth. He had come to abhor these meals, necessary for his survival, for his functioning, and yet an unheeding reminder of his solitude and his limited ability to thrive in this place where he had landed. The greens had been his only successful attempt thus far to grow anything edible, and they had been a welcome gift in the face of dwindling rations. They tasted metallic in a brutal way, like they were oozing iron or grown in a puddle of diesel.

Adam supposed that he ought to feel fortunate for being alive at all. Loneliness wasn't so bad compared to being blown to smithereens like his compatriots in the sky, though perhaps it was they who had been fortunate in the end, having been spared life in this strange hell. Adam had never experienced such profound loneliness. By all indications he was the only one to have survived. He had seen only one other ship break free from the strange suspension in the sky, but it was yet to be seen again. Adam wondered at the chance that someone else might be out there somewhere, though it seemed increasingly unlikely with the passing of whatever would be considered time in this drab world.

Adam had at first not believed the projected images of the pod’s exterior that ALICE had shown to him. It was once he had donned his suit and emerged from the pod himself that his eyes saw the truth of his circumstance. It appeared that they had landed in a gray desert, a soft dull expanse to the horizon in every direction, touching an unblemished sky, entirely pitch black without even a hint of starlight. Rather, the stars appeared to have been relegated to a circle directly above, roughly the size of Adam’s clenched fist held at arm's length. The constellations were crammed together in a dazzling dance, turning, rocking, drawing smooth arcs in choreographed pirouettes. The collective starlight glowed like a sun through a veiled, lace curtain, enough to light the odd world without exerting any great deal of presence.

In a moment of panic, Adam had cried out, but his calls had no reverberation, dying in the air or whatever filled the ether between gray and sky. Adam felt like he was falling down a well. His cries had turned to wails as he came to his knees, his back writhing like a mad cat, retching in sorrow. Alone, alone, alone- the word had echoed in his head as he cried heavy, lavish tears that clumped in the dirt around his hands, clawing at the strange soil. Adam had fought to calm himself, trying to quell the surge of panic that coursed adrenaline through his body in dreadful waves.

The tranquility of the world became for, a brief moment, a welcome reprieve, a silent retreat from his frantic mind, which already turned through a carousel of guilt and worry. For a moment he had laid down on the soft ground, pondering the punishment and the peace of this place. As he let himself rest, the gray had felt like a bed of moss that rolled off in soft, shallow hills all around.

When Adam had awoken he had quickly realized that he had become submerged, encased in the earth. The gray soil had weighed heavy around his neck and had panicked him. He had fought to break free of the viscous entrapment, like cement pressing him down. He had envisioned a sudden tide sweeping in, drowning him at the bottom of an empty sea drained of color and life. Breaking free of the overwhelming pressure, clawing his way back into the safety of the pod, he had realized that the sands of this desert were more like the waters of a slowly shifting sea. He had been fortunate to survive the strange riptide of these barren shores, at least this time.

It had come to seem that his only safe haven would be within the pod, and that this odd tendency of the world around him would impede his ability to seek out the slim possibility of another life. He had come to picture the others in his crew just beyond the horizon, their pods bound together in a caravan like a rosary, having reunited after a challenging entry and spending an evening watching a silent film on an old projector, laughing as one at the flickering screen.
ARE YOU ALRIGHT, ADAM?

ALICE's voice was a welcome break in the macabre silence, though Adam didn't expect this machine to empathize with his grief. What did they know about death on this scale? About life lived in sheer solitude? How could they given the nature of their creation? Adam knew that he was alone in this suffering. Looking down at the full bowl of gray greens before him, Adam wanted to throw it all across the room in a furious explosion. Instead, he held his breath and forced down another bite, the taste of acrid iron and acid rainwater lingering too long on his tongue.

A pale gray fog had sulked in, enveloping all of the nothing on the dust sea. Adam closed his eyes, exploring the ashy murk with his mind for the spirits of the past. He could sense them there - echoes, ripples, shadows of life that could have easily been hiding somewhere in the still fog, obscured from his wanting eyes. Resolute oaks and evergreens whose tips crossed the threshold between fog and cloud, stretching tirelessly toward an unreachable, unseen sky. Flurries of sparrows darting and flitting from limb to limb. An oafish pair of mourning doves who cooed contentedly and pecked at specks in the soil. A humble brown rabbit that had wandered boldly from its hollow.

Sensing his eyes suddenly filling up with wet and salt, he let his lids flutter open again. There was still a piece of him that yearned for that world - so deeply, in fact, that he nearly expected to open his eyes to find a squirrel atop the pod’s dome, gnawing absentmindedly at one of its treasures, an acorn recently birthed from the thawing earth. But there was nothing, as usual. No brazen hares or rodential midwives.

“You might not believe this, but I remember. I remember when this was all going to be gardens and trees.” He almost whispered the words, there more for himself than ALICE, to keep him from forgetting. And indeed, he hadn’t forgotten, not yet at least. In his mind’s eye he could still see burning autumns and the way that the pure whites of winter melted into verdant blooms and warm, lazy summer nights. It was thanks to those words - I remember - that he had held on even for this long. Them and this ritual of his, rising to sip his coffee and take stock of whatever was left. He finished the last of his bitter brew and withdrew upward to the greenhouse once again.

Greenhouse was, of course, a generous term - there was far more gray than green. The vegetation, so to speak, consisted of meager, naked, weary excuses for life. All around the room, the floor was covered with creation after creation, each flawed in some tragic way. Row after row of pots holding stems without leaves, or roots without stems, seeds that couldn’t bloom, or dust waiting to claim another attempt at life.

There had been some successes, though it seemed unkind to imagine a world brimming with these monstrosities. He had approached elation when a modest daffodil had taken off. He had felt giddy, watching it germinate and grow, barely able to sleep. He would wake and immediately climb the ladder to check on it, stepping away for his coffee only after ensuring the comfort of his darling daffodil. When it had bloomed, its petals were gray, a muted tone that managed to evoke yellow in Adam’s eye. In a moment of nostalgia he once had ALICE overlay color on the plant’s face. The vibrancy caught him completely off guard, and he was moved quickly to tears.
“ALICE, you know I shouldn’t waste energy on these trivialities,” he muttered, pressing away the tears that filled his vision.
I HEAR YOU, ADAM. NOTED.

When Adam next returned to the gray daffodil, it was hunched over, burying its face in the dust. From there, every daffodil he tried to grow was the same - sad in both color and stature. Eventually he moved on to other attempts.

Without a doubt, the worst of his creations was a type of grass that had emerged from his tinkerings. It had a faded blue kind of gray to its waxy exterior, though the neutrality of its tone hid its true nature. The grass had razor-thin edges and grew with tenacity, jagged glass sprouting in any dust where its spores happened to settle.

It angered Adam to have such an awful weed be among his most proliferative creations, and he had once kicked a container teeming with the stuff. A puff of spores erupted around him and filled the greenhouse. The cloud singed voraciously at his skin and throat, and he had burned red as embers throughout the laborious task of cleaning up the fallout. With regretful care, he collected nearly all of the grass and spores, jettisoning them from the pod, banished to torment something elsewhere. Still, a particularly aggressive container of the weed sat at the perimeter of the round room, covered in a pile of old suits to contain it. Looking at the unseemly hill still made Adam‘s eyes feel like they were burning, a memory seared in his mind and skin. For now, the new world promised to be free of rolling hills blanketed in soft green.

The proudest and saddest of Adam’s creations, a bare oak, stood just off-center in the room, positioned near enough to the open porthole so as to be seen while climbing the ladder to reach the space. Each time Adam clamored up from the dark living quarters below, he would train his eyes on its graybark branches, dark cracks that broke the clean image of sky projected on the dome’s interior.
Early on, Adam had focused his attention on modifying the simple acorn. It had been almost too easy at first, a pace of change and success that lacked the inertia needed to go on indefinitely.

As far as Adam could tell, there were core challenges in the environment that made life on the dust sea unsustainable. The first was the obvious lack of light – the dust sea was forever blanketed in near darkness. The sky overhead was devoid of stars, a pitch black quilt that felt smothering at times. The only light afforded to this world came from the dense collection of stars that danced and darted in the ring at the sky’s apex.

One of Adam’s first experiments in artificial selection had brought about an acorn that could germinate even in the lowest levels of light. He had begun with the classic genetic coding for an acorn and then manipulated it wildly, using all sorts of methods to knock the typical growth pattern off its tracks – beats of radiation and chemical baths. He would then bury these bruised beings deep in the dark underbelly of the pod, and wait impatiently for something to find a way to bloom.

Adam was faithfully determined in his mission. He had collected ample heaps of dust, scoop by scoop, and created a sandbox replica of the cruel world outside in the lowest part of the pod, the cockpit, turning the rounded floor of the bowl-shaped room into a broad, flat plane of gray grains. It was into the ominously still soil that Adam drove nearly every creation that he deemed sufficiently crafted and ready for release. And it was from the soil that the oak had emerged.

Adam was on his knees when he spotted the first sign of a stem, drawing his hands together to form a mound of dust atop another attempt at life. He nearly fell over. The oak had been among the first forms to emerge from the dust. Nearly every other deposit had stayed put, silently resting, rotting, in their roughshod excuses for graves.

The young stalk defied the death around it, standing tall and gray under the ultrafaint light coming down from the porthole above. Adam potted the young stalk with the utmost care, devout in his movement of this precious life. Slowly, rung by rung, Adam had carried the oak up to the more generously lit greenhouse, where it had stayed ever since, changing in slow, imperceptible steps.

The initial stalk, only ankle-high when Adam had discovered it, grew to reach his chest before it stalled. The gray branches were as thick as Adam’s thinning wrists and mottled with clumps of ashen leaves, bristle-edged and brittle. Adam had woken once to find the curious foliage littering the floor around the oak. Its bareness embarrassed him at first, before the feelings shifted to concern. Why the thriving plant had given up was beyond him. Despite every attempt at repotting it, no amount of dust seemed sufficient to entice it to extend itself further. Adam still kept it, despite its dormance, occupying its central place in the room, a statuesque reminder of his achievement in this abyss.

He often dreamed of it reawakening, breaking free of its gray cocoon, strips of bark molting and rolling off its form. What was underneath the gray was different from dream to dream - an earthen mauve, or electric orange, once even swirls of turquoise and maroon, an unexpected pairing that his mind’s eye carried on well after waking. Despite his desires, the oak remained resolute in its stillness and unremarkable for its grayness.

Adam’s fortitude continued to produce weak life in the dark pit of the pod, though nothing had ever come close in stature to the once elegant oak, his first true love in the garden. His successes had brought forth wilted, gray-green lettuce, and a variety of fragile white gourds desperately prone to collapse. An unfortunately loud sneeze had once punctured a sad lot of the gourds on the spot, causing them to rot before ripening. The greenhouse had become a much quieter, meditative space after that, with Adam and ALICE saving their dialogue for the living quarters in the space between the dark cemetery below and the dimly lit greenhouse above.

Despite often saving a day’s worth of dialogue, their discussions were typically short and direct. Energy was tight, and the ship required constant attention to avoid sinking into the dust sea. ALICE maintained a constant hum along the pod’s round exterior, holding the vibration in its lower hemisphere in such a way that the ship floated on the slowly shifting sands of the dust sea. The upper half of the pod stuck out of the dust, a broad, incandescent dome where ALICE devoted energy to taking in the minuscule light from the circle of stars overhead and transmitting the meager signal to the greenhouse within.

Inside the greenhouse, the domed ceiling was covered in panels that broadcast the starlight over Adam’s creations. It was certainly a grim space, but Adam found that there were times – usually after his coffee had kicked in – when his eyes would ease, adjusting comfortably to the low light. They would begin to paint the drab picture with vibrance and color, and he would walk the garden, running his fingers through the lettuce, seeing in them a deeper hue of green.

chapters.title: LIFE OF THE PARTY
paragraphs: Adam walked the circumference of ALICE’s circle of signal, their homing beacon pulsing like a heartbeat in his left eye. The dust sea was gray and dull like any other walk, a vast expanse of dust to the horizon. Mostly the dust sea was a tranquil place, and Adam found the outings could be almost enjoyable at times, marching in the near silence, his breath and beating heart the only sounds in his ear.

Walking the sea was a tiring task, somewhere between walking up an untouched hill after a thorough blizzard and standing at an ocean’s edge and feeling the sand slide out from beneath the toes. The shifting dust seemed to take on varied states depending on the trip. Sometimes it was loose, a thick slop that came to Adam's knees and belched disgustingly as he trenched through it. Other times it felt more packed, firmness beneath his feet that almost reminded him of home. There was often a preponderance of smog that hung over the so-called ground. On the more quiet outings it would stay low, hiding Adam’s feet when he stood still. Each step would kick up a flurry of dust that whipped and whorled at his legs. He left a curlycue wake in the dust, a long line that ran off as far as he could see, curving just perceptibly to the right. He would look back on occasion, using his own path to stay oriented.

Adam looked skyward, wondering whether the mad constellation of stars in the ring overhead held some bearings by which he could find guidance, but the astral codex appeared far too complex, a loose, ever-changing dance of flits and blips spattered and sprinting in bound loops. The whole of the universe was captured there, compacted into a single, fizzing static circle that punctuated the blank, black sky.

Adam’s path through the dust had taken a tangent from the homeward curve. ALICE caught him as usual, increasing the pace of the fluttering heartbeat in Adam’s left eye. The nudge pulled his attention back from choreographing the stars, and he corrected his course. The outings on the dust sea were risky, the sole hazard so far being the constant sinking. To be still here, to rest for too long, was to be buried alive. At times it shocked Adam how quickly the stuff seemed to accumulate around him, a relentless flow that required him to move continuously simply to stay afloat.

He would typically move at a jogging pace, fast enough to stay more or less at the surface, though he had to lift his knees high with every step and focus carefully on his breath so as not to overtire. He was grateful for ALICE, who could provide the homing signal he needed to go out and back - trips they called bungees. When Adam had first suggested the idea of exploring the dust sea, it had been ALICE who had been inspired by the idea of how they might do it.

The pod acted as ALICE’s central point - there was still the business of maintaining the ship, keeping it afloat, lighting the gardens - and ALICE was diligently focused there. Adam traversed the dust, wearing a pair of frameless glasses, little more than blank panes that floated before his eyes, suspended by subtle gold holds on his ears.

On each bungee, Adam would start first by radiating outward from the pod. Further and further he would trod until it was a hint of a bump on the horizon, maybe even less. Around this point the beating pulse of ALICE’s signal would begin to flutter and then sputter, a sign that he had reached the end of the tether. Here he would turn, moving relative to ALICE’s signal so that it beat with more fervor on his left side. He would then move counterclockwise - his preference - along the outer contour of ALICE’s reach. From his position, ALICE would volley the signal, a doubling of its radius that shot further out to seek a potential receiver. Adam wondered what he would even do if, after all this time, they had been heard by another ALICE from another pod. What would the strangers have to say to one another, he to the pod’s occupant, or ALICE to their counterpart?
“That was some ride, huh?” He could hear them whispering, holding each other close despite the distances dictated by time and space.

The eve of the departure had been largely celebratory, the crew brimming abuzz at the promise of discovery or potential annihilation. Their pods had been smoothed to the perception of perfection and sat all around the clusters of people, a wired chain of dark black pearls. Their surfaces caught and stretched the neon lines and dancing firelight that illuminated the festivities. Looking at the shadow of his face in a nearby pod, Adam felt barely recognizable. He had forgone shaving years ago, and it had been months since he last hacked blindly at the beard that grew so tirelessly, a coarse, brown bramble that often swallowed his mouth with its curled mustache. His hair, a modest auburn, hung longer than usual. His hand rose and pushed the locks out of his eyes, tucking them to the side and behind his ear.

“It sure is great when we can get together like this.” The sudden voice in Adam’s ear made him jump, in part because the words were yelled over the din of music and conversation. He turned to find himself nearly face-to-face with the ex-banker. The man was always taller than Adam anticipated, and he looked down with eyes that wandered in opposite directions, feeling the pull of some unseen action off stage and failing to see the person with whom he spoke. Adam wondered whether the man was addressing him or someone hiding behind the pods. He stood not quite facing Adam, nursing a neat whiskey alone and staring off into space two ways. He repeated himself, louder.

“IT SURE IS GREAT,” he took a beat to sip his drink, his eyes swirling back and forth between the cup and the hangar, seeking respite and finding nothing to hold. “When we can get together like this.” Adam’s eyes rolled. He was already repeating himself, unfortunately not atypical for this blowhard with grand ideas - the kind for which he’d break the flow of a good conversation in a sudden eureka that would buzz with great anticipation. And yet he never quite articulated his monumental visions, certainly not sufficiently to turn the heads of his peers. The group had grown comfortable turning their backs to him mid-conversation, sometimes quite abruptly, a shared, unspoken way to coerce him toward another unfortunate group.

Adam dreaded the thought of this man’s mind spoiling the contents of the chalice, but it brought him some comfort knowing that the ex-banker’s sad, repeating verse would be drowned by the ocean of others. He saw an opportunity to maneuver away, hopefully before hearing that melancholy song again.

A poet, a prolific storyteller extraordinaire and stocky toad of a woman, had meandered their way, eyeing their potential as an audience. Though small in stature, she commanded attention. Through her work she had honed the craft of the one-sided conversation. She wove complex stories, tapestries rich in threads, impressive in the sheer number of iambs crammed into every stanza. She was an endless river of telenovela tales, details broadcast without seizing into any space she claimed as her stage. She spoke loudly enough to be heard by just about anyone, always playing for those in the back row, yet she trained her eye on only one person, locking them in her crosshairs for a chorus, sometimes a whole song.

“It sure is great when we can all get together like this.” Adam caught the tired refrain once more as he pressed away into the crowd. Behind him he could already hear the poet setting off on a meandering tale with no end in sight, and he imagined the ex-banker squirming under her fixated gaze. Her intensity made nearly everybody uncomfortable, and the thought of the weary-eyed drunk attempting to keep up with her verbosity amused him.

Adam made his way to a corner of the spacious hangar where a particularly lively dialogue was taking place. A tall, lanky physicist in obscenely tall heels towered over a group of flustered mathematicians, a cadre of confident bullies that she loved to make feel little. She had strained her voice talking over the loud music, but Adam could make out her running theory - something about the hole’s interior being like a thimble with a broad mouth and a rounded dome belly pricked full of holes, themselves the mouths of ever smaller thimbles, like a tesseract that repeated down and down, worlds within worlds.
“Ad infinitum,” she shouted hoarsely, her voice practically breaking over the generous speakers that blasted one-hit wonders of bygone eras.

“Ya la bulla,” added an orator, a wiry man perched on the arm of an armchair and rolling his words beyond comprehension. The mathematicians nodded in weak agreement, enough to feel included without calling enough attention to be called upon. None could follow such eloquent words. Adam smirked.

The orator always made others lean in close when he spoke. His accent blurred garbled slurry syllables in a way that sounded vaguely Welsh, and though Adam could rarely follow his litanies and dirges, they always seemed to elicit the same passive posturing in those who heard his poorly poured prose. Adam wondered how the curls and clamor of the orator and poet’s swirling words would meld with the physicist’s hoarse hollers. He envisioned a cacophonous melody rich with sharps and flats, curves and jagged edges.

A nearby chorus of wailing children brought Adam back to the celebration. Nearby, the doula, swollen with pregnancy of her own, sat in a bouquet of faces notable for their sameness. By his account, they numbered in the twenties, the eldest already touching the tail of adulthood. For her, the night was mournful. Her eldest had elected to fashion pods of their own, opting for a solo ride over shotgun next to mother. Her cries were amplified by her toddler chorus, a classworth of children that would ride alongside her all in a single pod. She had been most concerned with the allowable number of passengers within each pod, curious why they had not opted to pack every pod twenty deep. After all, the doula had the capacity to call forth crisp genetic copies of any of the travelers from wombs wet or wired. She had done it for herself numerous enough times, opting more often than not to grow her progeny in external artificial wombs. She’d once had quadruplets on a whim in this way. That she had happened to be pregnant the old way at this singular moment was simply an unfortunate circumstance. She understood more than any the risk she carried with her fellow passenger.

Adam watched as the fool, who had been traipsed across the lap of her king like a lush throw, rose from his side and walked over to the doula. She held the sobbing mother in her arms until she had caught her breath again. The fool whispered something in her ear, a message hidden in the rabble of the event. Whatever was said calmed the hysterical woman, and she held what kids she could close with a broad embrace. Adam moved to intercept the fool, catching her on perpendicular paths that stretched across the expansive room.

“What did you tell her?“ Adam asked. The fool wrapped her arms around him with conviction. She was a head shorter, and her hair was like a lion’s mane. He could smell conditioner and champagne as she squeezed his torso, burying her face in his chest. She held herself there, taking a rest, a brief respite among the orchestral arrangement.

Adam caught his breath when she looked up, catching his eyes with her own. In her dark pupils he could see his own reflection, tiny and pink with neon glow. “Go alone or go together. Until we go through, we are blind to whom we remain bound.” She sensed the sadness in his eyes and threw her face back into his chest, pulling him closer into her warm embrace. “We might all land in Eden, or get tossed each to different worlds. We might spend eternity rocketing around like silver spheres in a pinball machine for all we know.“ She paused again, taking a deep breath before letting it out in a heavy sigh.

In a quick beat, she rose to her toes, brought her lips close to his ear and whispered, “I love you.” As she disappeared back to her king’s side, he wondered if she said that to all of the hopeless wanderers. Adam felt the cool blue eyes of the king on him from well across the room. He felt oddly exposed even among the crowd. The king was among the earliest of the wild-eyed refugees to set sight for the black hole’s elusive beyond. It had been he who had inspired the group to persevere, once even giving a speech quite organically in the round, evoking an era of Shakespearean tragedies that stirred something deep and core and ancient in the travelers’ bones, words that made the dark leap of faith somehow worthwhile. Of course the idea to deliver the rousing call to arms was the idea of the fool, one of many that she whispered into the ear of her king, planting ideas like seeds in his fertile mind. It was she with the vision. He was merely the voice.

Adam completed his arc across the room near enough to his own pod to catch his reflection on its surface, a curved copy taller and far off among the crammed party. He saw a warped form appear at his side - the physician.

“Are you sure I can’t convince you to reconsider?” The physician joined him in assessing the room. She thumbed at her nose ring in a consistent tic, tapping the steel omega that pierced her septum. In the grim neon glow of the party, the tattoos caressing her form seemed to bristle with life on her skin. She was referring to the chalice, with a question she had asked countless times before, and he delivered his usual answer - a resolute no - with a steely stare. “OK, I’ll back off, but if you’re even thinking of changing your mind, you better let me know soon. Once I finish uploading the rest of the poet, I’m calling it. The chalice is closed. No vacancies at this hotel.”

She held her eyes on him, prodding for a smile to find its way out of the bushel of hair that hid his face. While she was called the physician, she was no doctor in the traditional sense. She had painted illustrations for medical textbooks once, lifetimes ago, awfully real organs captured in ornate, immaculate detail, standing apart from the viscera that normally encapsulated them. They were hairless, skinless beings buried in thick textbooks meant to be left on bookshelves.

Advances in artificial intelligence had disrupted her career, snatching her skills and performing them in rapid real time, far faster than she could hope to race. She had adapted to a new role, one where she had retaken control over the machines. It began as a curious foray into learning about machine learning, and it had turned her world on its side. She remembered feeling elevated when she first thought to train an algorithm to the beat of a human's neural pulse, or the bass thump of their heart, or the tips and taps of blood cells bumping along one’s insides. It had been almost too easy setting up the system. She had laid prostrate in the belly of a moldy magnetic resonance chamber that she bought in an estate sale on some forgettable planet. Each run took a fair degree of energy and battery packs were in short supply, especially as she began on her own. Luckily it didn’t take long before she had a companion. The machine trained its attention on her for hours as she slept inside, learning the inner dances of her mind’s eyes. It grew increasingly aware of her, then able to replicate her language, eventually developing sufficient capabilities to converse from within the machine.

The physician dubbed it the chalice, as it held her mind like a cup of bold, blood-red wine. As she traveled the galaxy, she collected a wide array of weary minds. Soul after soul slept in the belly of her beast, allowing the thing to feast on their forms, running its analytical tongue along their brains and bones, licking at every drip of lymph. With each upload the chalice became more full, though it never seemed to overflow. The collective mind remained merged as one - one voice that commanded every talent among its myriad parts. Its mastery of physics jumped ahead noticeably after the physicist had her turn in the resonance chamber. Her upload had paid off immediately, as the chalice determined the importance of the precise spherical shape for the ships. The worried crew began honing their hulls immediately.

The doula had insisted that all of her children enter the chalice, and they had each complied, especially the infants who slept with sweet shallow breaths inside the howling magnetic monsoon. When the doula herself had emerged from the cocoon, the chalice quickly reached a key new insight. The algorithm determined that the most optimal approach to facing the unpredictable opposite side of the black coin was to flip the coin as many times as possible.

“It’s a numbers game,” the physician had explained to the others. They sat among heaps of half-used batteries, a handful of stragglers dressed in dirt and cast-off rags, carrying whatever they could carry with whatever life they could muster. At that point their resolve was still meager but their numbers were growing stronger. They lived day by day as best they could on an ex-exoplanet knocked free of its first orbit. It had approached the black hole, sending its residents fleeing to wherever their life rafts might land, refugees on a sea of stars and near endless space. To the world's surprise, the rock had fallen into an elongated elliptical orbit that threw it from end to end of the galaxy.

The travelers in their well-worn clothes huddled for warmth in an abandoned hangar, watching each season as the looming black hole above grew closer and closer, until it was an immense presence that swallowed the whole sky. Each time though, the ominous god backed down from its posture, sliding off to a near pinprick at its farthest point. Over and over, the black hole consumed and filled their sky, and all the while, they continued to pour more of themselves into the chalice and hone ever more of their smooth vessels.

When Adam had found his way to this place at the edge of the abyss, the chalice and the hangar both brimmed with life. The first of the poet’s files had finally finished uploading, and the chalice immediately started producing stanza after stanza of clunky poetry that smoothed out after the orator’s contribution. He and the ex-banker had a slur and blur to their inner monologues, soft edges that smoothed the hull of the chalice’s voice.

The physician had at first invited Adam quite haughtily to join his mind to her creation. By this point, the chalice was wired into every available pod, a collective network of hard drives that held the flowing contents of the collective mind. The travelers planned to cut the cords shortly before launch, distributing its contents evenly among the vessels, a seemingly fair divvying of the talents inside. Though Adam had contributed his pod’s memory to the collective effort, he had been resolute in his decision not to place his mind into the hands of the machine.

He found the whole thing flippant, feeling as he did that everyone who put the machine’s eyes on them was surrendering before the fight began. What irked Adam about the whole affair was that one of the pods was going to be left behind on the bleak rock, humming along on a lofty supply of battery fuel.

The left-behind pod had been a monument for the doula’s lost child, meant to be ninth in line in her family. Tragically the doula’s creation emerged stillborn. She had honed the sphere herself, soaring sobs and withered whimpers filling the hangar as she worked through the endless night. The physician had offered solace in her way, an extension of the chalice that could sit within the form crafted by the shattered mother. Adam pictured this copy of the chalice, turning through page after page of its unlitigated library of learned sojourners, trying to reconcile some pattern in their tangled web of threads.

The party erupted into a united countdown as a salvaged disco ball was lowered from the hangar’s riblike rafters. It hung with splendor, casting squares of bright white across their gray faces, filled with brief hope for their tenuous mission over the cosmic horizon. Before they reached one, the rafter holding the frayed tether snapped, sending the disco ball hurtling to the floor. It shattered on impact, sending shards of light and glass every way. Adam knew in that moment that their odds crossing the threshold were slimmer than a razor’s edge, and when the sad voyage fell over the point of no return, that lone copy of the chalice would be left, a sad scourge of broken mirrors of people, digital zombies feasting until their batteries ran out. To him, the choice was clear, seeing as he did the true potential of things, and he stood by it, resolute among a puddle of broken glass bits.
ADAM, IT’S TIME YOU COME HOME. YOU’RE TRAVELING TOO FAR THIS TIME.

Adam came to on the dust sea. He had nodded off again, an issue becoming more common than he cared to admit. The dust was up to his waist and he had to kick hard to pull himself out again. ALICE’s beacon was faint in his eyepiece, but there was enough to regain his bearings. As Adam made a direct path homeward, toward the center of his circle, ALICE waxed prolific, an unusual behavior he noticed when their attention strayed too far from the pod.

ARE YOU SURE I CAN’T CONVINCE YOU TO RECONSIDER THESE  BUNGEES? IT’S A NUMBERS GAME, ADAM. YOU KNOW THAT. EVERY TIME YOU CHOOSE TO GO ALONE INTO THIS DIFFERENT WORLD YOU FLIP A COSMIC COIN.YOU’VE SAID YOURSELF THIS IS NO EDEN, AND BY MY CALCULATIONS THE DUST GOES DOWNWARD, AD INFINITUM. MY SENSORS HAVE DETECTED NOTHING BELOW, SO UNTIL WE GO THROUGH, WE ARE BLIND. I AM NOT SOME SILVER SPHERE ROCKETING AROUND IN A PINBALL MACHINE, ADAM.

Adam listened with aplomb, hearing the poet’s rambling nature in ALICE’s response and seeing where this thread was leading. ALICE’s beacon heartbeat fluttered, and their next words came in low, practically a whisper against Adam’s ears.
I LOVE YOU, ADAM.

Adam laughed at the thought of freshly popped champagne shooting in joyous streamers across the room, as he climbed the dust sea to his lonely home. Stepping over the threshold and back into the pod, he whispered a weary reprieve from his own memory: “It sure is great when we can get together like this.”

chapters.title: THE BLOOM
paragraphs: Adam trudged along his arced path, navigating the dust sea once again by the beat of ALICE’s digital heart. He hummed a loose melody on this bungee, less a coherent tune and more a happy hodgepodge of half-found tones. For the moment, cadence and chords didn’t matter. Adam was writing jazz, his hands tapping rhythms on his hips, snapping to an imagined high hat with just the right hiss in each hit. The invisible path ahead for once curved to the right thanks to a happenstance decision he had made to circle the pod clockwise. Adam was feeling adventurous, bordering on lucky. How could he not, when ALICE’s bass drum beat beckoned him home to a field full of fragrant flowers?

He could see himself now, stepping into the pod door and shaking the dust out of his ill-fitting suit. The extra space between his body and the fibers meant more room for dust to accumulate, but it also made it easy to shake off the ancient garb. He would pause on the spot beyond the threshold, waiting impatiently for the little storm to settle before making his way to the ladder in the room’s center. He might consider a dip into the graveyard, a quick check for any unexpected blooms, but most likely he would ultimately decide to climb skyward, near naked and still caked with dust. Besides, it had been some time since he had last planted his haphazardly fabricated creatures in the teacup world below, and what he did in the garden above was nothing short of magnificent, a masterpiece of his own making.

He would smell them the moment that he stepped into the pod, a perfumery that sang sweet dulcet tones into every breath. The searching scent would have found every nook of the empty air during his outing, uniting the interior of the pod with a filling, singular stew of scintillating sensation. One that would bathe him upon entry. Rung by rung, he would swim up the ladder, diving deeper toward the source of the smell. His angel, the oak, would peer down from overhead, its grisly gray branches cutting the sky and reminding him of the potential in his perseverance. He would press his body against the oak’s scarred trunk, a subtle and sacred touch that would ground him in the reality of this moment. The view from the central point in the room would feel familiar and full, for he had spent many meditative beats there, unable to step away and retreat down the open porthole, instead eying the creations that covered every surface, a packed bouquet of electric yellow daffodils that had come to wash over the whole room. Eventually he would walk the perimeter of the round space, picking a meak harvest from the barely edible gray greens that had been displaced to the periphery by the more welcome bounty. All the while he would find himself barely able to rip his gaze from the tether of his beloved beauties. Their faces would glow in the starlight, pleasant yellow butter melting into warm bread, and Adam would feel fulfilled.

It would only be after perhaps hours of this glorification of his golden floral fold that he would regrettably retreat to his humble cot. He would have trouble finding sleep again, and he would ask ALICE to tell him the whole story once more, as he searched for solace in slumber, the hum of their whirring parts moving behind the panels, hiding the graveled rumble in his gut. It would be costly, expending precious energy on this delight, but Adam would be willing to pay this toll. To compensate, ALICE would quiet the hum along the underbelly of the pod, allowing the ship to sink slowly, temporarily, into the depths of the dust sea. Faithfully, ALICE would oblige Adam’s wish:

THERE IS ONLY STARLIGHT AND DUST HERE, AS FAR AS WE HAVE COME TO KNOW, AND YET WE NOW HAVE SEEN THAT THIS LIGHT CARRIES MORE POTENTIAL THAN FIRST MEETS OUR EYES. THIS DISCOVERY CAME WHEN YOU CHOSE TO SPLICE TOGETHER GENES OF EVERY FLOWER WE COULD FIND IN THE RECORDS. YOU SAID YOU WERE CREATING A MOSAIC OF BASE PAIRS, AND DESPITE YOUR POORLY FORMED HYPOTHESIS, WE WERE SUCCESSFUL IN THE PURSUIT OF NEW LIFE.

I RECALL YOU THINKING THAT YOU HAD CUT YOURSELF SOMEHOW, WHEN YOU FIRST CAUGHT SIGHT OF THE EXPERIMENT’S OUTCOME. THAT INITIAL SUCCESS - A POPPY, RED IN COLOR, THAT YOU DESCRIBED AS BURNING AND BLOODY - WAS INDEED A SURPRISE GIVEN THE BOMBASTIC METHODS YOU TOOK TO INTRODUCE THE NUMEROUS GENETIC BLUEPRINTS TO THE FLORAL HOST.

WE REMAIN UNCLEAR ON THE MECHANISM BY WHICH THE FLOWER ACHIEVED THIS FEAT. YOUR NOTES HERE ARE SPARSE AND MORE GRAPHIC THAN INFORMATIVE. YOU SAID THAT YOU BELIEVED THE FLOWER TO HAVE BEEN FLOODED WITH EVERY PIGMENT KNOWN TO MAN, A PRACTICAL ARK FOR THE GENETIC CODE OF A DIVERSE FIELD. YOU SAID YOU COULD HEAR THE HUM OF BEES SEEKING THE FLOWER’S STAMEN, YEARNING FOR PETALS EATING UNSEEN COLORS OUT OF THE AIR. SADLY, THE BURNING, BLOODY POPPY FAILED TO SEED BEFORE EXPIRING. THE NEW SPECIES WAS EXTINCT AFTER ONLY ONE GENERATION.

YET KNOWING THAT WE HAD A PROOF TO YOUR PRINCIPLE, YOU CONTINUED DEVOTING TIME TO THIS PURSUIT. DURING THIS PHASE YOU WERE SUCCESSFUL IN PRODUCING A ROSE CARNATION, A CHARTREUSE VIOLET, AND AN EMERALD MARIGOLD, THOUGH NONE OF THESE WAS SUCCESSFUL IN PRODUCING VIABLE PROGENY.
YOU THEN REQUESTED THAT I EYE THE PLANTS’ CODES AND DETERMINE A WAY TO ASEXUALIZE THE PROCESS. “DAMN THE BEES,” YOU SAID.

TO THIS END, THE DAFFODIL EMERGED AS THE MOST OPTIMAL SELECTION FOR THE GRAND TASK. MY HISTORY OF ANALYSIS ON ITS GENETIC CODE PROVED FERTILE FOR RAPID ADAPTATION. IT WAS THEN WITH FAITHFUL ITERATION AND REITERATION THAT THE DESIRED RESULT WAS REALIZED.

It would be around this point that Adam would finally drift into slumber, comfortable in the knowing of this story's end. He would dream of the buttery smear in the room overhead, rolling in it, bathing in it, until his whole body was covered in glitter-gold pollen and his skin glowed in reflected yellow remnants of starlight. At times the field in his mind would glow so bright that he could only close his eyes, basking in the glimmering darkness. Even in dreams, their vibrance was too great for his vision. It would only be when his dark dreams no longer satisfied him that Adam would erupt out of bed.

Up the ladder he would fly, brimming with anticipation and near fear, an expectation that he and ALICE’s creation may have changed while they looked away, or worse - that it never was at all. He would sigh in relief at the top of the ladder, as his vision grew full with the field. Until he had coffee in hand, his mind would dance on the coin that flips between the dreaming and waking worlds, allowing it to turn and turn and turn, and Adam would rest cross-legged under the oak’s reach, orbiting through mind-filling darkness and something like yellow, blinding and loud.
ADAM, THE STARS ARE BLOOMING.

It was the unusual choice of words - blooming - that caught Adam’s attention. His mind returned to his place on the dust sea. The world was mostly flat, as usual, aside from the occasional ulcers that blistered meekly skyward or the dark sinkhole pockmarks littering the landscape. His gaze turned to the fluttering snowstorm of stars encircled overhead. To his untrained eye, the madness appeared as mad as always. “What do you mean, blooming?” he queried.
I AM… NOT QUITE SURE.

There was a beat as ALICE processed what they were seeing, reviewing every photon, every bit that flowed from the complex constellation to the ship’s surface and through the chalice. ALICE’s tone took another unexpected turn.
SOMETHING IS COMING. YOU SHOULD RETURN, ADAM. NOW.

Their direction was rarely so direct. Adam understood their capacity to see what he could not, so he began making his way back, ALICE’s tireless heartbeat balanced evenly on his lenses. He would worriedly cast quick glances skyward, seeking some change. “What’s going on, ALICE? I don’t see anything.”
IT’S GROWING BRIGHTER, ADAM. DO YOU DETECT IT TOO?

Adam looked around, trying to gauge the level of starlight that hung in the air. Perhaps the gray did seem brighter today, though Adam had no real days to which he could draw a comparison or connection. Early on ALICE had maintained some record of the old time, holding a faithful metronome beat to stay bound to the paces of a world worlds away, but in the end it was simply wasted energy. Adam now marked the passage of time by the withering and wrinkling of his body, a reminder for which he needed no reminding.
THE ENERGY IS MOVING, ADAM.

Adam felt his hairs stand at attention. There was a low hum building on the dust sea, a fearsome sound he recognized from before. His memory raced through a ticker-tape flurry of moments. The dark swarm inside of him, the battered fleet suspended in space, the feeling of plummeting into this dark gray hell, so blind, so naive. “ALICE, where is that sound coming from?” He quickened his pace to match his heartbeat, racing though still not quite fast enough.
IT’S COMING FROM THE STARS.

Adam’s eyes jumped to the heavens in time to catch the event. A cluster of stars had been plucked like grapes from the thick dark vines of space. They fell without fighting, dropping with grave abandon toward the hole in the sky. They raced to fill the dark void that held their unplucked peers.

In a flash, the first of the stars slammed into the mouth above. Adam was suddenly staring into a sun. The blinding light burned so fiercely in his eyes that he slammed his lids shut, as hard as he could, though even then the immense power glowed through them, the fluorescent red all that he could bare to see. The low hum grew with sudden alarming force into a brutal and deafening roar of static filling every frequency, flooding each airwave with an ocean of noise, freight trains wailing on shrilling horns, squealing into their stations, children smashing keys on an out-of-tune piano, shrieks and roars, bellows and groans, crumbling gravel and splitting concrete over bursting bombshells, fireworks and forlorn wails, a spectacle somewhere between awe and angst.

“ALICE, WHAT IS GOING ON?” he yelled, barely hearing himself over the sheer volume of cosmic radio turned to full blast, caught between stations, the knobs broken, sound swelling until the speakers clicked and sputtered, failed vessels for the signals in the static. Adam could sense ALICE responding but the words were lost in the dissonant symphony. The homing pulse melded with his own frantic heartbeat in the blood red filling his eyes.

Adam attempted to squint against the light, to suss out ALICE’s signal from the noise of his own heart, but his pupils tightened into miniscule dots, two bits of ink barely on the page. He could make out only faint, mighty structures that had emerged quite suddenly in the world. Mountains rose from the once-flat sea, gargantuan mounds topped by epic monoliths that stretched endlessly upward into all-consuming light. Adam felt impossibly small, trapped in a valley between these monuments.

“ALICE!” he cried. “I’M LOST.” A swirling storm of gray clouds rolled down from the mountaintops, fully blinding Adam. He was at the mercy of the storm’s endless slurry of light and dust. It filled his nose with a smell like gasoline, metallic and distant. He coughed up gray paste, bland and bitter, an oily film lingering on his teeth. He trudged forward in what he hoped to be the way back. He felt dangerously far from the safety of the pod, adrift on a maddening ocean furious with monsoon.

All around, he envisioned sinkholes spinning into torrential whirlpools, ravenous mouths hungrily consuming the all-consuming dust. The pressure of the storm slammed against his back, a punch that beat the air from his lungs and crushed his heart from behind, threatening to launch his vital insides right out of his chest. It compressed him relentlessly, a sinister, unwanted touch that enveloped and ravaged his tired body. Adam gasped a dreadful lurching gulp of dust and was swallowed alive by the dust sea.
I’M HERE, ADAM.

Adam’s mind tried to make sense of the madness. He had barely considered what else, if anything, might fall in with him, though here in the mire he found himself wondering why it shouldn’t happen. What grand laws could possibly hold the mammoth universe in an endless harmonious dance, whole and safe beyond the reach of the hole’s crushing grip? Adam, along with any other unfortunate survivors, had found a way through after all. Why shouldn’t the stars be free to be tipped from the cosmic scales, thrown breathlessly off-balance? What balance was there, really, in stillness? Were the stars not all constant in their changing, steady in their churning, blowing the bellows to light the living embers of the universe?
I’M HERE, ADAM.

Only ALICE’s voice gave Adam any sense of his place in this abyss. He beat at the dust like a half-dying whale, grabbing for breaths and screaming them back out, trying desperately to stay afloat among the rumbles of the white-washed sky. A wave of torrential smoke crested and crashed upon him, pressing him back into the darkness below. There was another flash of sky, and his face felt the surface again. Everything prickled with static, heavy and humid with the weight of the momentous storm. The sky was cracked down its seams, rigid rivers of dust spewing onto the mountains of the sea.

He spied his ship again, its familiar domed top pulsing black and white with the same rhythm as the one lens that remained in his eye. He cried out for help as loud as he could, but the sky roared with thunderous laughter, drowning out his calls as another monstrous wave rose up and pushed him under yet again. The world around him began to pound and hum with shocking vibrance, and he surfaced again, swallowing mouthfuls of dust. His eyes were blinded by a piercing light, and he coughed out a scream, fighting desperately to catch ALICE’s attention before more thunder could follow the celestial lightning. His arms clawed at the heavens, though he saw only ceaseless light.
I’M HERE, ADAM.

Adam felt his body rise up from the dust, the sea hanging heavy in his bones. He fell, exhausted, onto the metallic hull of the pod, as the furious sea pitched back and forth. His eyes rolled in his head, the din of the storm screaming all around.

He remembered smoothing this hull himself, by hand, so many eons ago. He could still see his face, blurred in the ship’s reflection, shrouded in a pool of seamless light. His mouth was moving but he couldn't hear his own words. The dust fell hurriedly, filling his eyes, until there was nothing but dust and darkness all around and the relentless clamor of the world somewhere above.
I’M HERE, ADAM.

Adam picked at shallow breaths, his world growing ever darker, becoming ever more aware of an omnipresent Ohm, a vibrating hum that sent shivers through his spine. The feeling almost tickled, a caring touch against the ruthless storm beyond the caress of darkness. Adam allowed himself to rest, at ease at last, floating inward like a star drawn to a nebulous black hole. The pod door opened, and inward Adam fell, dust and all.

chapters.title: FERTILE GROUND
paragraphs: Adam’s hands moved the dust resolutely in spite of his growing reservations. His persistence of late had crescendoed to a point approaching masochism. Sleeping, eating, even the occasional bungees beyond the pod door - all had been stricken from his schedule, now singularly focused on his current experimental endeavor.

The end goal had seemed simple enough at first, but nothing, as Adam had come to accept, was simple about creating life in a lifeless land. His hands met under the dust and rose up from the growing basin, carrying out a small mound and tossing it across the nearby miniature landscape. He felt like a child at play in a sandbox, only this game was far more serious than sandcastles.

He pressed his fingers again down into the dim soil and felt something firm pressing against his palm below. His hand closed around the thing and ripped it out with a swift tug. In his fist were the vestiges of an out-dated experiment, a sad hybrid flower of some sort or another. Its petals were withered and turned inward, their colors swirling in vibrant tie-dye. Adam looked at it with somber curiosity. It appeared to have bloomed underground, out of sight, wasted effort and energy making beauty for no eyes to see. This had been a persistent challenge for Adam, the tendency of life in this place to fail at finding its way to the surface, a natural result of the lacking light from above and the relentless pull of the dust below.

With a resigned sigh, he tossed the grotesque creation to the room’s edge. It was time now to make room for something different. He continued pulling at handfuls of dust, drawing them from the growing crater, eventually lowering himself into the shallow grave and jettisoning the dust up and over the edge. When he felt satisfied with the work, he laid down in the pit, folding in his arms and legs to ensure he could fit comfortably.

Lying there, his cheek resting in the base of the silken smooth dust bowl, Adam wondered what it would be like, gestating in the dark dusty belly of the ship. The idea seemed incomprehensibly foreign to him, with his old, inherited ways of reproducing human life. Yet Adam, with the knowledge of ALICE, was clawing ever closer to realizing the unthinkable.

Adam had awoken after the star storm with dust caked in his eyes, half-buried in a mound of the stuff. Though the ship was turned right-side, it was clear that it had been rotated on its axis. The litter all around the living quarters was piled in a pointed heap at the room’s edge. Adam felt conspicuously like a piece of rubbish, sitting among the pile of junk, especially covered as he was in a heavy blanket of dust.

Looking around it was apparent how meager his life had become. His cot with its fraying linens had been soaked by a pot of cold coffee, another notable stain among the oil and dust that soiled his sheets. Scatter stacks of papers covered the corner, analog ideas trapped on the page, certain to be lost in the annals of time. Rising from the shallow, ashen grave, Adam picked his pipettes out of the pile, placing them next to his dissecting microscope, which stood with resolve right where it had been left. He felt grateful to an Adam from ages ago for having the wherewithal to have bolted the scope to the workbench. A break in its lenses would have rendered him blind to the tiny worlds normally hidden at his fingertips.

Thinking about his work caused Adam to jolt to sudden attention. He practically flew up the ladder, moving as quickly as he could despite his tired muscles. Looking up through the porthole, he knew immediately that something was wrong. The gray projection of sky was whole, unmarred by the jagged branches of the gray oak. Adam knew before seeing what he would find in the garden, and yet the sight gutted him harder than he had expected. Every pot, every plant, every piece of his garden had been thrown without regard to the edge of the circular room.

“No, no, no…,” he muttered, grasping desperately in his mind for a glimmer, any semblance of hope somewhere in this jumbled mess. He ran across the room, his hands plunging into the rubble of broken vessels and shriveled flowers. All around, the faces of his beloved daffodils peeked out from behind shattered clay pieces or from under hills of dust that held them hard to the ground. Embedded in the chaos was the oak, inverted and odd, its branches buried deep into the haphazard horde like roots, and its roots protruding embarrassingly out into the open air. The oak seemed to beckon to Adam, offering a consoling hug that felt still, gray, and cold.

His loneliness suddenly became unbearable, and Adam buried his face in his hands. The teardrops in his eyes welled into rivers, and he sobbed openly and honestly, his shoulders heaving with weary gasping breaths. His achievements had been destroyed, a cosmic wiping of the slate, back to square one. Worse even than that considering the time already lost, the sheer energy costs that had been paid.

Adam’s hands returned to the crumbled remains of his floral flock to pick up one of his colorful creations. Running his fingers across its stunning yellow petals, he felt growing clarity on where he had gone wrong. “I can’t do this alone.” The vulnerability he felt in saying the words was shocking, and he was immediately overcome by a deep sense of something like catharsis. He realized how limited he had been by his own bullishness. His stubborn adherence to growing new life on his own and to the discovery of old life somewhere on the sea had prevented him from seeing the truth of his circumstances. There was a faint hum as ALICE processed what he had said aloud.
I AM HERE, ADAM.
