ALICE’s words were nearly lost, as Adam was already on his way, moving with new direction back towards the porthole. Their choice of words felt robotic, repetitive, overtly simple, and his attention slipped past them without pause. He half slid down the ladder, feeling cavalier about injury after having nearly been smothered by the sea. He passed through the living quarters only briefly, digging a crowbar out from the cluster of cast-off belongings on the room’s edge. He then continued down the ladder into the graveyard, his feet meeting the metallic floor of the cockpit for the first time since he had filled the room. The dust and its hidden dead treasures had fallen to form a dune in one edge of the inverted dome’s periphery.

Adam walked to the opposite edge of the room, pressing his fingers against the panels in the wall, seeking the holds he remembered to be hidden there. When he found a break in the seemingly seamless array of panels, he wrenched in one end of the crowbar, then threw all of his weight against the lever. Despite his waning size, the bar turned on its fulcrum, and the panel popped out, clattering gracelessly onto the hard floor and sliding towards the room’s center.

The space behind the panels held artifacts of a bygone era. The nook revealed by Adam’s prying held items that he had once carried across the galaxy. He half-recognized an ancient backpack, stuffed full with camping gear he could never need. His eyes fell on a familiar pair of hiking boots, worn through wear after wear to the contours of his feet. He picked up one of the boots and turned the heavy, odd thing in his hand, feeling somehow irreverent for pulling it from its place of rest. The sole still held fast to acrid bricks of mud, pieces of an earth impossibly far away. He could see himself in that other place, in some distant time, kicking these boots off so haphazardly, not even bothering to untie them first. The lashes were pulled tight in taut knots that seemed unwilling to relinquish even a hint of their hold.
Adam returned the shoe to its partner’s side and moved on along the round wall, his hand again seeking purchase among the panels. ALICE spoke up in the silence unexpectedly:
I AM HERE, ADAM.

Adam felt the bite of unintended irony in the statement. What ALICE was, and more so what ALICE was not, felt glaringly obvious after the grave incident. He scoffed to himself, causing a clump of dust and saliva to come up in the back of his throat. He spat the gray phlegm away, his mouth tasting again like he had licked tire marks burnt into asphalt. He found another opening in the wall and again used his crowbar lever to reveal the hidden cache.

“Do you ever feel aware of your limitations?” he queried. He half listened as a fan whirred somewhere in the ship, though ALICE made no response to his question. It seemed that they were at a loss for words. His eyes scanned the suitcases and luggage that filled the niche before him. Nearly none of it belonged to him. Once, on a rock long ago and far away, certain others among the crew had learned how lightly Adam was traveling and had approached him with offers in exchange for cargo space. Here he stood, once again face-to-face with the physicist’s outsized set of stilettos. He must have looked incredulous when she had first proposed the idea of him carrying her things over the event horizon, but he had put up little resistance when she had loomed over him in her way.

Behind the next panel he discovered book after book of the poet’s writing, stacks of moleskins and composition notebooks that still smelled vaguely of graphite. She had been wary of relying on the chalice alone to maintain her works for posterity, so she had opted to hand-write a redundant body of her literature. Her diligence in the completing of the task had evoked images of monks in Adam’s mind, working slowly and methodically with ink and quill. Seeing the volumes preserved before him felt like a testament to the human hand and a pointless rebuke of the printing press.

Adam continued around the room, prying panel after panel from the wall, leaving a wide cavern in the room’s side that made the space feel lop-sided. ALICE continued to offer their assistance, seeming to feel the need to fill the silence however they could.
I AM HERE, ADAM.

Adam may as well have had ears still full of dust. ALICE’s voice elicited at most a slight nod of his head. Each time they spoke, the bitter bland taste of the dust sea swelled again in his mouth and made his stomach turn. By the time Adam found his intended target, he had nearly worked through half of the room’s perimeter, ripping piece after piece of the ship off the wall. Standing in a shallow puddle of dust, he let out a humble gasp when the next panel fell away.

His eyes fell on the styrofoam containers, packed tight in the already crammed cargo space. His frown bent just enough to be called a grin, though any feelings of joy were tempered near instantly by Adam’s blooming doubt in their capability to realize his lofty dreams. His eyes closed, just for a moment, and he caught his mind’s eye alighting on an image of the fool. She stood on the opposite side of the room, and yet he could sense her champagne scent. She opened her mouth to speak, perhaps simply to breath, but in a sudden whorl she disintegrated into dust that dissipated into the dark corners of Adam’s memories. His eyes opened again, falling squarely on a box labeled OVA, and he whispered his determination once again: “I can’t do this alone.”
There was a beat as ALICE listened to Adam’s resolution. The whirring fan stopped for a moment, deepening the silence of the empty graveyard.
I AM HERE, ADAM.

The water flowed faithfully from the spigot, belying the limited pool that hid in the walls of the ship. Adam caught the precious liquid in a makeshift flask holding a heap of clumpy powder, earthen and bloody in color, like a fresh scab. As the flask filled, Adam swirled the mixture, setting loose the dark powder into the solution, muddying the waters until they were virtually opaque. It was a cocktail of amino acids and antibiotics, rich and wholly indigestible in its complexity.

Adam had once attempted to consume the broth, but his insides had nearly fallen out in the ensuing upset. His body seemed to have forgotten long ago how to handle such robust nutrition, or perhaps he had forgotten the feeling of one’s microbiome being eliminated by powerful drugs. Whatever the case had been, the tidal waves of sickness had caused him to lose far too much of his necessary fluids as he ran over and over again to the pod door to vomit into the dust.

The ochre soup neared the mouth of his vessel, and Adam closed the spigot firmly. He then moved, smoothly, slowly, across the bare floor of the living quarters. Reaching the ladder, he gripped the container tightly in one fist and grabbed at the rungs with his other hand. The muddy mixture sloshed worryingly with each step down, as Adam moved carefully and clunkily, one rung after another. As he found some footing again in the graveyard, he allowed himself to once again breathe freely. His feet sank more deeply into the dust thanks to the newfound weight that he carried.

With shifted attention, he began the methodical task of bringing the undrinkable coffee-colored fuel across the room to the freshly-dug hole. He had fallen once before, spilling the flask’s contents disastrously into the dismal dust. He had nearly cried at the wasted water and dense nutrition sinking into the ground but thought better before allowing himself to lose another drop of fluid to useless tears. It had taken only one fall for him to rethink his approach, and here he was, many many trips later, transferring his weight back and forth like a heron searching for fish in the murky waters at its feet. He would wait for the dust to settle after every step, feeling in the faux earth enough firmness to press down with his weight. As the dust compacted under his sole, the other foot would rise, streams of dust flowing between his toes. His step would scan forward cautiously, finding a distance that felt like progress without being overly zealous, and only then would he bring the foot down to find its next hold. The process, though painfully slow, had a way of filling the time, whatever that meant in this timeless place.

Eventually, with sufficient patience and prowess, he would reach the basin’s edge, where he would add to the contents of the amniotic sac cradled within. The sac, an artificial womb, was a cobbled-together creation, at its essence a bubble of precariously thin plastic sheets held together by a redundant mix of sutures, glue, and electrical tape. Each sac could hold gallons of the dark, mud-brown liquid when the making of the makeshift womb went well, resting like an oblong sow covered in earth. It was unfortunate then that the wombs were so prone to leaking. In the more fortunate cases, there would be only a bit of seepage at the seams, a dung-colored dew that Adam would dab away diligently. On more than one occasion, though, there had been catastrophic bleeding out, typically starting at the entry points of ALICE’s wires. During these incidents, Adam had tried hopelessly to salvage whatever he could from the mess, eventually burying the whole failure in dust and cursing ALICE’s intrusions under his breath.

This wiring, however, was critical to the process, as it allowed ALICE to monitor and manipulate the activity inside of the womb. There were twelve connection points in total, arranged more or less at the vertices of a somewhat round, somewhat polyhedral, inverted cupola wedged into the ground. It was through these points that ALICE performed a wide variety of tasks. They maintained a near constant temperature, notably warmer than the surrounding space, and measured the balance of electrolytes in the murk. If the womb was successful in the initiation of gestation, ALICE’s manipulations would go on to support ever more complex functions, stimulating cells to move and neurons to fire, a dynamic cellular show of puppetry without strings.

The more mature wombs had been literally buzzing with activity. Adam had listened with reserved concern each time. To his ear, the hum of each womb was a reminder of the major energy costs he was incurring through this experimental expedition. Each successive attempt had drained the ship of progressively more of its critical reserves, power and water devoted to and wasted on these failed endeavors. So far, there had been nineteen attempts and nineteen failures. The reasons for each misstep were varied, but they all amounted to the same thing in the end - one less egg from the limited cache and one fewer human to join him in the new world. Adam wondered at this point whether he was expending energy on something that was altogether pointless.

Hoisting himself out of the hole, he sat for a moment on its edge, staring down into the dark muck held below. His mind filled with the memory of yellow - a sweet, glowing gold that warmed him from within. The dust under his hands felt cool and empty, as always. There was work to be done, energy to expend, and he rose to his feet, vessel in hand. Though his destination was dark, the path to it was clear. “Keep going,” he muttered, returning to the task at hand once again.

chapters.title: THE PEARL
paragraphs: Adam gazed down with a mixture of wonder and worry into the open grave that held the artificial womb. He batted away at incredulity and a nagging sense that what he saw before him was only a shade in a dream. The form in the womb had grown up rapidly, emerging almost suddenly from the primordial ochre broth. What had at first been a dark and amorphous shadow in the brown ether had extended with voracious rapidity into a man perhaps the size of Adam, though it was hard to tell, wrapped as he was in a fetal knot within the plastic sac.

The amniotic fluid was thin around the man's form, and his face was pressed close enough for Adam to make out his features. What had surprised Adam first was the hair on the man's head. That it even grew at all in utero was unexpected, but even more astonishingly, it burned a fiery earthen orange that Adam had not seen since life before the entry. One of the doula’s many children had auburn hair of this sort, and she had stood alone among her siblings’ crowd of flat, jet black hair. Like the girl, this man's face was awash in a flurry of freckles, a mosaic of melanin that seemed to change in the low light. The figure’s form was surprisingly toned, having never left this quiet slumber in the belly of the ship. Through ALICE's electrical stimulations, the muscles of this body would flex and relax, tone and soften. At times the movement had become so strong that Adam feared it would rupture the fragile womb.

Even now, he could see movement in the face below. Behind closed eyelids, the eyes were watching something with fixed intensity - a battle or ballet - drawing attention in every direction. Adam wondered what was being shown on the screens of this dreamed theater. A soft grin on the face below suggested that he might awake and tell Adam some great secret, perhaps some key to unlocking the doors of his own mind. But then, what could this person possibly know that would not have been shown to him first?

A sinking sensation returned in the pit of Adam's stomach. He had spent so much of his attention towards this effort, and here it was before him - apparently successful. Yet he found himself wondering whether he might be better off aborting this before it went further. He tried to envision ripping the wiring from the womb, cutting through the seams to let loose its contents before closing up the grave, another addition to his crowded cemetery. ALICE’s voice broke through his half-hearted daydream.
IS THERE SOMETHING THAT YOU NEED, ADAM?

Adam didn't feel sure enough to answer. He had been pushing down this path for so long that he barely remembered what had set him off to start. This person growing below - was he an assistant? Another pair of hands to help tend to the withering greens in the garden above? Or perhaps a companion - another perspective who could fill the quiet chasms of the lonely pod? Adam wondered whether he simply craved the feeling of something unexpected, some unpredictable addition that might shake him loose of this gray eddy.

Already he anticipated the coming risk of doubling the population in these tight confines. They would need to double down on food production to start. A challenge, given that Adam anticipated needing to teach this person nearly everything. Would they start with speech or with the sciences? Would this being’s brain even be capable of higher thought? Or would this sad soul be born with an unraveled mind at the start? Adam’s worry grew, and he gnawed nervously at the edge of a nail, studying the subtle features of the face, trying to determine whether he was looking at an eventual ally or enemy. The closed eyes continued watching some hidden, dynamic dance as Adam tried to figure the quickest way he might get this stranger up to speed.

Admittedly Adam himself had slowed quite a bit as he fought the entropy of the gray wasteland. Even the grand genetic manipulations that had brought about this new life had remained largely out of Adam's capacity. His abilities as a geneticist were impressive on human scales, but it had been ALICE’s direction that had ultimately carried them this far. Adam had done his best to maintain a sense of the atomic happenings at play as he assembled viral vectors per ALICE’s instruction. He knew they had seen success with early gestations after providing the eggs with new capacities, drawing shortcuts in metabolic pathways, splicing signatures into spaces between genes where the machine could flex the code open and closed, turning unseen knobs from within the cellular cogs of the contraption, unseen means to explore the capacity for life in every way that the genomic blueprints could allow.

Each attempt at gestation had added to an ever more complex cocktail of codes, a muddled storm of viruses set on transforming each sacrificial egg into a viable embryo that could reach fruition in this barren world. Only one critical component had remained out of ALICE’s abilities - the genetic complement needed by each haploid egg. Adam had reluctantly attended to this need, a mix of sadness and shame washing over him each time he allowed his mind to wander to the smell of champagne and the feel of a cork popping, knocked loose by the bottle’s brimming.

Together, Adam’s and ALICE's contributions held terabytes of informational potential, though admittedly his flailing cells seemed primitive and blindly incapable alongside the surgical manipulations of the viral machinery. But both components were needed for the eggs to grow beyond their punctate stasis, and it gave Adam at least some sense of balance in the process, as if there was some leveling of a playing field. But watching the flitting, frantic movements of this mysterious figure left Adam unsure about the worlds ALICE might be conjuring within this new mind. Whatever was being seen within that blossoming consciousness was sure to make this gray wasteland seem like a veritable ashtray by comparison, and it left Adam wondering whether he could be enough for this person. Could he sustain them? Or would they instead wander off into the gray desert, succumbing to the pull of dust over that of the pod?

Adam’s mental abacus began to tabulate the resulting loss he could face down this path, his eyes following imagined beads that slid left and right. He summed immeasurable time spent assembling unseen DNA pieces for half-understood gestations, slowing his speech to give language to this life form, showing this being how they could make life from what little they had here in the gray. He then envisioned this person - his companion - venturing off alone, disappearing over the horizon’s edge without him. Adam recognized the magnitude of the improbable calculus for only a moment, before casting aside the abacus in his mind's eye, breaking free of the gravity of as of yet unrealized outcomes.

Adam stood over his progeny, now nearly fully grown according to ALICE. In one hand, he rolled an onyx pearl, pensively turning the dark orb with his fingers, spotting glimpses of himself in the modest spherical mirror. His other hand held tight around a utility knife, his thumb resting on the trigger, ready to release the hidden razor blade inside.

That the person curled up in the faux womb at Adam's feet had grown to term was both expected and unexpected. In a sense, things had gone according to plan, though Adam recognized that that plan had been largely dictated by ALICE. What was surprising was that it had worked - this time at least. After so many failed attempts, Adam had found himself often simply going through the motions, making mixtures according to ALICE's guidance and thinking little about their purpose. The act of action had become the focal point of his bland life in the gray, and despite the absence of success it had kept him in motion when he wanted to stop, to rest, to sink. His eyes cast furtive looks around the room at the numerous mounds in the gray soil, the fruits of his labor that never ripened, a field of neverborn lives. A clutching feeling gripped Adam's throat as he considered the tolls paid in working so stubbornly, so myopically at this toil. He swallowed hard and pulled a strong breath in through his nose, sighing away the tightness as his mouth fell open.

There were times when Adam thought he could feel his own pearl, moments when he could swear it was dislodged from its resting place deep in his trachea and instead rising up into his voice box. He would be unable to speak when this happened, practically mute until his body's internal machinations seemed to place the loose piece back where it belonged. Adam's pearl had been inserted by his father, a man of myriad faults who had insisted that Adam install the artificial organ. Adam could still see the black pearl in his father's calloused hand.

“The atmosphere on this planet is bound to collapse at some point. It could happen any day now.” He had been young, barely in his teens, but already his father had inundated his mind with the images of collapsing exoplanets across his travels.

The man was a specialist of interstellar travel, a rover, though what his exact specialty was had alluded Adam in his youth. What Adam had known was that he had been all over the galaxy, an experienced frontiersmen of the exoplanets. His time aloft on the cosmic sea had left him rich with history, a witness to whole worlds, from freedom to famine, populaces rising and falling, the ebb and swell of masses as they fought to survive on the burning embers of their homes.

Adam’s young mind had grappled with the grim stories in the stars. Already he had witnessed the brutal march of his own planet's atmosphere in collapse. It was a slow, nearly imperceptible change - oxygen dropping out of the air, waning ever so slightly each day, eventually reaching a tipping point where the planet could no longer sustain human life, so it was predicted. Yet the populace had remained, willfully blind to their own slow drowning. The gasps and strained sighs of the people had become the backdrop against which life pressed on.

The creation of the pearls had been borne out of this challenge, by those with the foresight to care. The seemingly simple spheres held a tightly regulated collection of superfluids suspended within a supercharged semipermeable steel. When placed within the trachea, the orb held the capacity to mediate the conversion of carbon dioxide into oxygen, acting as the complement to the body’s own breath. Each pearl was sufficient to sustain the entirety of a human life, simply recycling breath until the body's other faculties gave way. They had been a boon to space travel, allowing the movement of human life more readily into unexplored niches before costly terraforming efforts could wash foreign worlds in breathable air.

Adam's father had been given a pearl when he had become a rover, and it was through his connections that he had gotten his hands on one for his son. Those hands had gripped at Adam's jaw, holding him firmly in place, then gripping the pearl between thumb and forefinger before Adam’s open mouth.
“It will hurt, but we’ll move quick. You breathe in hard when I say.”

Adam’s whole body had trembled with anticipation and fear, but his eyes had stayed locked on his father's gaze, the points of their pupils at the ends of some unseen line. Before Adam could respond, his father had pressed the pearl into his mouth. It was big enough that its edges had scraped at his teeth. Adam had nearly choked but already the strong hands of his father pressed the solid orb further inward. Adam grabbed at his father's arms, his eyes filling with tears, but the man's fingers had pushed brutally on the back of his throat, choking him from the inside.
“Now.”

He had demanded, and with that signal Adam had lurched at last for a breath, his diaphragm pulling taut as his body yanked on the imposing presence, demanding it to move further inward. He had gasped at the air, a fish freed from water and fighting for life.
“Breathe, damn it!”

At last the pearl had found its place, moving past the bottleneck and dropping into its pocket within Adam's chest. His neck had throbbed with every heartbeat, but he had immediately felt the difference in his breath. The feeling was an easing. No longer was each inhalation a strained pull at the air. Already he had felt his heartbeat slowing, his mind clearing, his body humming with more vigor. In the end Adam had thanked his father for the gift.

It was later that same year that Adam's father had departed. The mission had been dubbed an NCB - no coming back. Adam’s father, along with a crew of other rovers, intended to hop the ride of a lifetime.
“We're riding on a comet! It'll take us across the great divide into another galaxy, Adam. This is the next step for our civilization’s survival.”

Looking at the excitement in his father's eyes, the sheer joy of the promise of finding far-off dreams, Adam had felt confused. That he and this man shared the same mix of genetic stuff and yet were bound to live lives wholly apart had baffled him. The truth though was that Adam's birth had been an unexpected, unplanned event that had anchored his father to this exoplanet far longer than originally intended. Having seen Adam to near adulthood, the man had felt finally freed to pursue his own path once again. His departure had been unceremonious. He had risen and left in the dark hours before dawn, leaving Adam with only a list of directions for managing the disposal of his left-behind belongings, scrawled quickly on a torn-out page signed by the ring of a half-drunk mug of tepid black coffee.

When Adam next saw his father, they were two entirely different men. Adam had lived through the better part of a lifetime. He had grown into himself - a gruff man, keen and lean with a coarse beard that he chopped back on rare occasion, its colors running auburn with patches of gray and hints of amber, a bramble that hid his smirks and sighs. In his lifetime, cheap automation had come to displace most human-related work, setting off catastrophes in economies across the galaxy, as the masses lacked the means to attain wealth and were given the bare scraps to survive by their lords and leaders.

Life had been meager and humble, and Adam had spent his time studying the wealth of information available at his fingertips - a vast digital network that ran across the galaxy. The network was a boundless space wherein human and machine minds had melded and proliferated in every vulgar way, producing a septic pool of noise and advertisements and banners and pop ups and emails and meeting requests and yet, in that noise, Adam knew where he could find information.

He studied genetics, then robotics, then botany and melittology. For a while he had fostered generations of tardigrades, though he had grown tired throwing their frequent baby showers and funerals. Throughout that fascination he had learned how to mold the genes of his tiny compatriots, and he had crafted whole lineages of super-tardigrades, setting them loose one day on the grass outside, free to live on with the ability to withstand immense radiation or live in the vacuum of space.

Like the super-tardigrades, Adam had done very well in the odd new ways of the world. His penchants for modification - genetic, epigenetic, cybernetic - bore him many clients. Folks of every kind would make their way to his parlor, where he would concoct a cocktail of fancies - viral and bacterial, mechanical and seminatural. Into these strangers he would inject these mysterious solutions, and each time they would be accepted willingly, each patient in this odd clinic afflicted with some unforgiving burden.

Whether his treatments worked or not was harder to say. There had been no formal clinical trials, no controls or placebos. For the most part he had great anecdotal data to lean on. Granted, one woman had broken out in a relentless scaly rash, and a couple others had a bad case of diarrhea for a few months, but he had a much, much longer list of satisfied clients. He had given old bodies fresh cartilage, reversed the mental decay of neurodegenerates, and brought sight to a blind child. He had even cured some remarkably nefarious bouts of cancer, an increasingly common plague in the era of normalized migration through highly radioactive space.

As his life had passed, his reputation had spread, and his visitors had come from increasingly foreign worlds, seeking some change in themselves to live, in some cases to survive. It was for that reason that he had been unsurprised when he opened his door to find his father. The man was far different from the idea of a parent that Adam had come to hold in his mind. He was frail, almost brittle, his hair whitened and thin.

Adam had already brought the waif of his father into his parlor, had already sized him up for typical late life treatments - erectile dysfunction, prostate cancer, cataracts - when the faint resemblance to someone familiar clicked into demanding realization. Adam had nearly wept when he saw the man for who he was, grabbing him in a sudden, unexpected embrace, but the small creature threw him off with a cold shrug. The man had leaned closer to stranger than father, but he had been in need of a place to rest, and Adam had felt no resolve to deny him.

The reluctant roommate had moved into Adam's modest abode and with time had shared his story. He had ventured with the rovers onto the comet, as planned. Their course had been set clear across the vast expanse between their galaxy and its nearest neighbor. Yet somewhere in the ether their navigational systems had broken down. The stars of our galaxy had grown distant but no others had ever come close in their place. They were surrounded by an endless sea of distant worlds on a comet that appeared to move, but was motionless by all of their measuring devices. They had remained in this limbo for decades, their minds withering, their fears blooming into madness and mutiny.

As the last of the rovers remaining, Adam's father had grown accustomed to the silence of loneliness. Even living with his son, that comfort in the space between words had been his haven. The man had been discovered by an astro mining corporation. A mining drone had strayed well beyond the galaxy's edge and had found something fairly worthless compared to its typical cargo - a lone human in a useless ship. The comet on which he'd been found had at least offset the costs of returning the stray to his next of kin.

In spite of his years the man had become less of himself. He had often thrown fearful looks over his shoulder, as if he was certain someone was sneaking up from behind. Adam had woken once to find the man standing over him in the darkness, a vacant angry look on his face. He had taken to listening in on Adam’s conversations, casting judgments with barbed glances and short words. His callous interjections had become choking, a litany of vitriol that littered his life, day in and day out. With time, Adam had grown to see the man as venomous but weak, clutching to life simply to spite death. He grew ready for the inherited version of his father to expire, purposefully withholding the benefits of his wares under the guise of limited supplies, to which the man had barked.
“Selfish! You are selfish!”

The words had rolled the pearl from Adam's chest into his throat, stopping him from speaking, from barking back at the frothing old dog. And then the moment had passed, the old man had gotten on with his mundane card play, and Adam’s opportunity had vanished.

A day had come finally when the old man was quite certainly near death. He had become bedridden, debilitated by some infection and a weakening heart. In a moment of pity, Adam had given an experimental mixture to his father, a swirl of genes from potatoes and sugar cane, bits of foreign bloods plucked from a rich tapestry of wanderers. To his surprise, the man had come alive in his final days. His rough demeanor melted away, revealing a simpler, blissful being beneath. He took to plucking at the strings of a guitar on the porch, muttering soft songs about blackbirds pecking at the dirt. Adam had found him in a heap on the earth one morning, a full mug resting on the porch’s edge, its steam turning circles as it disappeared into the air.

The ochre sac was pulled taut around the man's form, pockets of brown broth nestled in the niches of his joints and curves of his face. Against the gray of the dust bowl, the body and the encasing had the appearance of an oyster ripped from pluff mud. Adam rolled the onyx pearl again in his hand. It was dark as the sky beyond the pod, the pearl that had once belonged to his father, soon to belong to this being. Who was he to Adam? A son?

Sure, in a genetic sense, they were related, intertwined, but what beyond that was shared? Adam wondered what had been seen in the prenatal dreaming of this creature, what pictures ALICE had painted in the molding of this mind. He could smell the sweat in his palm holding tight around the knife, salt on acrid aluminum. He needed to use it to cut this person free from their slumber, a sort of sideways C-section with no umbilical cord. Adam realized this person would have no navel.
I AM READY.

ALICE's voice broke him free of his turning thoughts. Adam wondered whether he was ready himself. He moved carefully down onto his aching knees and, before he could fall back into thinking in circles, punctured the plastic membrane with the tip of the blade. He chose a spot where the ochre broth had pooled, ensuring a comfortable distance from the man's body. The opening around the blade released built up pressure and oozed the viscous brown sap down the clear plastic. The stuff smelled of wet soil and felt slick to the touch. Adam pressed a finger into the hole and pulled on its edge, sliding the tip of the knife in to draw a tangent across the crumpled plane of the sac. It surprised Adam, the ease with which the blade ran through the seemingly rigid film, the way it reminded him of wrapping presents for some extinct holiday. He realized that he had forgotten the fragility of these things.

When the encasement had been cleaved the length of the man's curled body, Adam opened the two sides, casting them away like curtains flanking a window. ALICE had instructed him to move quickly here, and thankfully his role was clear. He took the man's face in his hands and turned it towards him. Seeing it in the low light was like looking into a puddle during a rainstorm and catching a somewhat familiar form gazing back. Bowing down, Adam placed his mouth over that of the strange reflection and blew a forceful breath.

Instantly the breath of life was gasped, grasped by the man. Adam felt the pull of his diaphragm, his whole body bending, the air drawn out with intense force. The unexpected strength left Adam with the sense that something was being lost in himself, and he pulled his face away in stunned awe. The man was sitting up, his eyes open, awake. He held Adam's gaze with his own, then broke the curious stare with a darting glance to Adam's hand. Adam remembered the onyx pearl, what he needed to do with it for this being to survive. The man gave Adam a knowing glance and nodded calmly. Adam was baffled by how lucid he seemed. He had anticipated the likelihood of a newborn brain in this mature form. Yet as the pearl rose to meet his lips, the man opened his mouth wide, as if already familiar with the brutal ritual. To Adam’s surprise, the man had baby teeth lining his gums, oddly small in the adult mouth.

Adam pressed the pearl inward as his father had done for him, finding pause as it met the back of his voice box. He recalled the feeling of that moment, the certainty that he was being strangled by the man. Now, this stranger was placing his hands on Adam's forearms, calmly and firmly, and reiterating his readiness again with his eyes. Adam sighed deeply, readying himself for the act and, nodding once with a semblance of finality, pressed the pearl with two fingers hard into the man's throat. Their eyes watered, Adam’s with pitying tears, the man's with quieted winces. Adam pressed, pushed, willed the pearl to find its way, seek its niche next to this fellow's heart. Still it did not budge. Adam's heart raced, fighting to keep ahead of the fear that this man might suffocate here before him. Adam's strained grunts crescendoed into a crying wail as he pressed with all of his might, giving the pearl no other option but to move, and with a sick slopping inward wretch it was gone.

The man's eyes flit across Adam's face, studying it up close like a detailed painting, Adam staring back in wonder. After a moment in the shared silence, the man rose to his feet. Adam fell back stunned. His hand tightened around the sticky handle of the utility knife, ready to strike at the stranger standing over him.
“I am Evan,” he said, extending a hand. Adam reflexively grabbed it and was hoisted to his feet.

“Evan?” Adam muttered the name. Who had chosen it? Had it been ALICE? Adam had been steeling himself to rehabilitate this person’s supposedly atrophic body. No name had been discussed with him. It seemed ALICE's interjections in the womb had done wonders in bringing this body to term and beyond. This Evan had been born with sound body and apparently sound mind. Even now, Adam could see the awareness in Evan's eyes as he looked curiously around the room.

“It's just like I dreamed,” Evan said quietly. Adam’s brow furrowed in confusion, and Evan turned to meet his confused look. “ALICE knows this place intimately.” Without skipping a beat, Evan marched across the room, his feet leaving new prints in a line that bisected the space. Adam watched warily as Evan ran his palm along the room’s curved edge, like he was reuniting with a lost lover, feeling the contours of the form at his fingertips. “All this time, there's been something I wanted to do. When we finally met, that is.”

Adam's skin bristled. His thumb pressed into the trigger on the knife, readying himself for Evan’s next move in this deceptively timid game of chess. Evan pulled out a panel of the wall with a pop, the gray hard veil wide between his hands. He sat it in the dust with care, then hoisted from the dank storage alcove an artificial evergreen. He pressed it down into the dust, turning it around the faux trunk’s axis, encouraging it to find firmness in the graveyard. Then dropping to his knees, he ran his fingers up under the lowest boughs.

With a click, the tree was alight. Ruby reds and sequin golds, streaks of wavy cerulean wove through the evergreen needles. The vision danced feverishly, casting unexpected color all around the once lifeless space, lighting their faces in a prismed sea. Adam dropped the knife absentmindedly into the dust, bathing in cool beauty long ago forgotten, feeling as the pearl rose and fell against the pounding of his wonder-stricken heart.

chapters.title: SEARCHING FOR ANOTHER
paragraphs: Adam sat easy on the edge of his cot, his hands nestled around a nearly finished mug of cooled coffee resting in his lap. For once, he was reticent to get on with his endless list of tasks, which at the moment was topped by another bungee on the gray sea. Evan sat across the room, curiously watching something at work in the microscopic realm. He was hunched over, staring down the eyepieces of the dissecting microscope.

The strained arc in his neck reminded Adam of the physicist, craning low to hear the squawking of the mathematicians. Her height had a way of distancing her from their dialoguing, but that characteristic shape showed that she was always keen to be a part of it, no matter how far off she might seem. Adam wondered for a moment if his neck arced that gravely when he worked. He lifted his chin self-consciously, picturing himself old and wearied, bending, bowing to the gravity of the calling grave.

“How about one more? For the road.” Adam rose to his feet, shaking his fog of memory and fiction, and crossed the room to begin pulling on his gear. Evan’s eyes turned to Adam knowingly, a coy half-grin growing on his face. That hint of joy alone made Adam feel his heart swell in his chest and tears well in his eyes. Evan seemed not to notice.

Already his gaze had drifted somewhere else. He looked off towards some unseen corner of the round room. Adam could see the dancing of his eyes watching rich, invisible scenes, memories from his dreaming in the womb. Adam had become enamored with the vivid detail with which Evan recounted these scenes shown to his developing mind in electric amniotic morse. Though Adam knew that ALICE was using her input to guide Evan’s growing neural networks, he had not even come close to envisioning the rich cinemascope that now resided in this mind across the room. Evan’s eyes locked on something, and his grin grew bigger on his face.

“I know just the one. Let’s see: I remember waking up. It was night, somewhere deep in the middle. It was dark, so dark that I couldn’t see anything. I felt my head resting on a pillow and a light cover, so I was in a bed, and I was pressed up against…someone. I can’t say who they were because I never saw their face. We were close, side by side, and our arms were interwoven. We were like interlocking parts, complex, curved puzzle pieces that fit no matter how we turned. I could feel their breathing, slow and peaceful, sleeping beside me in my blindness. I buried my face in their neck. The bridge of my nose fit the nook of their shoulder. I could tell they were awake because their toes danced with mine down under the covers, warm in their wandering. We rested like that, a comfortable knot, held tight in the darkness by our own gentle retying, pulling one another closer with each quiet snore. I felt like I was there for an eternity, drifting in and out of dreams, dreams within dreams. Eventually my ear caught the distant chirrup of some bird’s song. I rose from my tangle and felt my way towards a window. My hand landed on a curtain, velvet on my fingers, my body knowing where to move in the black void. I suddenly had goosebumps. I was thinking about the smooth skin of the one still in the bed across the room, velvet all over my body. Even still I could hear their faint breaths, sighs and soft gasps suggestive of the drama in their dreamscapes. The last thing I remember is the way the sun truly blinded my eyes when I opened the curtain. The rush of light flooded the whole room, bathing me and them alike in pinks and golden orange.”

Adam up to this point had been listening with bated breath, fully prepared to go but lingering happily at the pod door. The thought of a pouring tidal wave of light reminded him instantly of the star turning, of the instantaneous mountains that had dropped out of the sky. Suddenly the impending bungee loomed heavy in his mind, darkening the edges around Evan’s bright story.

Perhaps sensing Adam’s mind running adrift, Evan addressed him directly after a beat of thoughtful silence. “Should I share another one? For the road?” Adam allowed the idea to turn in his head, contemplating what it would be like to hang up his suit and forget his devout ritual of searching the dust sea for anything - for life, for another sign of humanity - and stay here instead with Evan, hearing as he wove elaborate tales like a skilled artisan working a loom with seamless movements. He considered whether the secret to happiness might be simply to close his eyes and picture a different world - drawn from the past or future or both or neither - anywhere but this moment really. Maybe then he’d discover something more than limitless dust beyond the pod door.
Adam shook free of his dreaming and gave a weak smile to Evan. “Dreams within dreams, eh? Sounds like there will be plenty more to share.”

Evan returned the smile with added energy. “You know it.” Adam felt restored by the promise. This person, sitting right there across the room, would be here waiting for him when he returned. This person, with stories in his eyes and brilliant ideas in his fertile mind just waiting to be pulled free. This person, tragically destined to live on in this barren world after Adam expired. Adam knew that he needed to use what time he had to be present in his life, before the day came when he was absent from his life. Yet the call to attend to humanity’s remembrance required that they continue looking outward, continue walking off the horizon to see whether the edge dropped or eased down, continue seeking some island on the dust sea where they could plant roots. And Adam knew that the ship did not carry sufficient resources to give Evan a companion. He reserved some smidgeon of hope that he might find one for Evan, floating in their pod somewhere on the vast plane of the dust sea. Adam grabbed onto that flicker of hope. He smiled a half-grin that mirrored that of Evan’s and stepped through the pod door.

The arrangement between the two of them had been apparent from the start. Adam first trained Evan how to work in the garden, the two of them alternating turns with the dissecting scope, both burying their wares in the graveyard, both tending to creations in the garden above. Their work would guide where they resided within the ship at any given moment. The solitary nature of the solo microscope meant that most conversation in the living quarters required proper intention, a motivation from within Adam to find a point of shared interest as he sat on their shared single bed. They had taken to switching shifts on the cot, and Adam woke often to Evan working quietly across the room, usually humming something vague and familiar, with a warm pot of coffee just brewed.

The sleeping arrangement had elevated chatter with ALICE up to the garden. There, they would converse on their needs of the moment, sharing insights on how to fold proteins into different forms or forge paths fusing genetic code to place meaning in their messages. The two would then take their musings into the core of the ship to tinker before planting in the basin below. The dust in the graveyard had come to cover ALICE’s speakers there, making it the quietest place in the pod.

In the silent emptiness of the ex-cockpit, the only consistent sound was the hum of ALICE running waves of energy over the ship’s hull, a repetitive practice that had kept them afloat all of this time. It was a rolling thrum, waves calmly lapping on a beach at dawn, the lolling strum of fingers on a guitar, persistent accompaniment without melody, a singer forgetting to share the words. The noise had quickly been tuned out by Adam’s ears. Its consistency and calm had been the backdrop against which they had worked in the graveyard, the buzzing of unseen drones working at the flowers that hid beneath the still dust.

Slogging on as he was on this bungee, Adam ached for the stillness of the ship’s interior. On this outing, the sea was especially slippery, his steps sliding from under his weight as if we were walking on thick ice. He wondered whether the gray sheet held any unseen cracks beneath, ravines and caverns waiting to gobble him whole after just one misstep. He kept his eyes trained on his desired path, holding ALICE’s beating pulse in his left eye as he moved.

He waddled awkwardly in places, his weight leaning forward to counter the unpredictability of the slick steps. He tried for a moment to envision Evan on an outing like this, how lost he would feel, how tired he would be after a calm, sure-footed life spent inside of the pod. Adam chuckled at the thought of Evan returning to the pod after a bungee, kicking dust out of his boots, his face flushed pink with the weary work of the walking. Adam might have just started a fresh pot of brew to dripping. He would rise from his seat at the scope and help Evan take off the foreign garb, dust spilling in heaps at their feet. “What’d you think?” Adam might ask, and Evan’s eyes might meet his with a solid, knowing glance. They might hold something scary and sad between them in that moment, a shared awareness at last of what awaited them in the end.

Adam’s balance on the dust shifted suddenly and he caught himself just before falling, instead standing uneasy and yet steadily still for a breath. When he felt measured once again, he continued along the arced path, his mind returning to the idea of Evan on the sea in his stead. Adam wondered what it would have been like, if it had been Evan in the end who had discovered some lost member of their crew on an outing? How queer an idea it was to even consider other survivors, but then again, Adam had made it this long by the support of ALICE. Why shouldn’t any of the other refugees have seen the fruits of their labor? Certainly another ALICE would have also figured out how to remain afloat on the dust sea. Unless Adam was simply the lucky one, the only one who had rolled elusive snake-eyes on the stochastic digital dice rolling around in ALICE’s programming, the sole survivor who discovered a happenstance path forward on the path of life. Perhaps the other passengers and their ALICEs had simply traveled down another trail, one that ended unfortunately beneath the dust. “I found someone,” Evan said, stepping through the pod door in Adam’s mind. “Tell me everything,” Adam would say, his eyes alight with intrigue and caffeine.

“I knocked at the pod door. To be frank, I wasn’t sure what else I should do to get the occupant’s attention. I had just walked right up to their pod. At first I thought I was misguided and had stumbled back to our own, but the beating of ALICE’s beacon in the eyepiece was certainly not coming from it. At first there was no sign of life. Nothing but the hum from the other pod’s base. I could feel it, vibrating even through the dust, even through my boots. It was a low, deep thump of bass that tickled my legs as it vibrated up my spine and into my tingling ears. The door flew open and this man was standing there. He seemed stunned but his eyes were fresh and focused, locked right on me. Before I knew it he was embracing me. Warmth radiated from the narrow space between us. He held on for a long time. A very long time. So long, in fact, that we started to sink before we realized. He invited me in and showed me everything. ‘It’s so great,’ he kept saying. He was ebullient that anyone else had survived. He had anticipated going on with only ALICE until they simply couldn’t any more. He said they had taken to writing songs together at first. ‘When we can,’ he would add. He had a conspicuous dripping of sarcasm in his voice that I think he thought was lost on ALICE. He whispered to me later that their lyrical contributions had grown stale with time. They had eventually dropped the duo act, and he had taken up golf. Apparently he had played once, in his banking days. He had fashioned the basin of the ship into a sand trap, where he practiced his chipping. He was quite good, I found, at nocking a ball across the room with ease. It was unfortunate that there was no rolling plane of green to catch it! For putting, he had set up a series of obstacles in the central chamber. Static barriers and ramps of all kinds. His precious collection of golf balls was all over the place. They littered the corners all around the room in electric neon colors that I had never seen before in this waking world. In the top of the ship, he practiced his drives. You could see cracks running all over the screens of the pod’s interior. The ALICE there assured me that he was developing his form quite nicely. He agreed that it was much improved from all the practice. When I mentioned you and our ALICE, his eyes lit up. I think he knew you because he was keen to reunite very soon. ‘We can all get together,’ he kept suggesting. His excitement was burning through his eyes. They started to drift in different directions for a moment, and I asked what he thought you would want to do together, after being apart for so long. That’s when he showed me what he called his pottery studio. It was a collection of lumpy bowls and clunky woven patterns, all made from this monotone ochre clay. He said it was all free-hand, since he had no wheel to work on, but he swore it was delightfully meditative. ‘Like this,’ he said. And then he sat right there and grabbed a ball of malformed clay from his heap. He quietly pressed it into a form like a cup. I remember watching the way his fingerprints pressed over one another, melding in the setting skin of his making.”

Evan would then hand Adam the weakly formed vessel, a gift from a man far off on the dust sea, barely holding together under its own heavy weight. Adam would try to picture the ex-banker’s face again, try to find with his mind’s eye the image of his form, swinging an iron in an overconfident sweeping arc, casting splinters and shards across the screened sky.

Adam hoped that perhaps they might find someone more useful to their expedition. He considered the mottled crew of miscreants who had been foolish enough to jump together through a blind spot death trap. He wondered who might be equipped with the best skills based on the dire circumstances they had discovered on this side. Who could crack the code of the sinking sea and find a way to seed life?
“Tell me everything.” Adam could hear himself saying the words, maybe in his head or out loud, his eyes alight. Evan would have just kicked off his boots, trails of dust forming at his feet.

“I ran the moment that I realized what I was seeing. It was just a blip on the edge of the horizon, and it just so happened that I looked over my shoulder. I would have walked right past it, but I had this intuition, like my mind knew that there would be something if I only looked. And when I did, there it was. I nearly sprinted to it, the dust was so firm at that moment. I was certain that I was going to scare the ship’s occupants because I was practically banging on the exterior until I found the door. I knocked for a while, and at first there was nothing. Then the door flew open, and there was no one there! It felt unceremonious but I saw myself in. Stilettos littered the floor around the door. They were studded and striped with heels like terrible speartips. I heard a voice from above, so I worked my way around the clutter, pushing aside linens hung around the room, and eventually I climbed up to the level above. I found a woman there, reclined on a heap of things fashioned into something like a chaise. She didn’t even get up when I came in. She was dressed in sweats and barefoot. Her hair was loose and tousled around her head. At first I ran to her side, thinking that she was injured or too weak to stand, but she pushed me away. She was insistent that she had authority over herself, despite her apparently waning energy. As if to confirm that she was not yet finished, the ALICE assured me that they were well on their way to deciphering a new theory to explain this place. I remember feeling giddy to learn what they had uncovered. The woman added that they had no choice but to find a new theory. ‘Because what else is there to do?’ she said. She could barely keep her eyes open, but she kept talking. I wasn’t even sure she knew I was there, but it was clear that she needed someone to hear her still - me, ALICE, or maybe it was just for her. She rattled off complex formulae and a slew of technical phrases that flew over my head. As she spoke, the screens in the room would come alive, graphs and charts, long equations over complex diagrams that made my eyes spin just looking at them. What I gathered from her explanations is rough at best, if not completely flawed, but it seemed to me that the challenge they sought to solve was one centered in oversimplicity. She said that seeing the interior of the black hole had collapsed all of the probabilities into something concrete. She had seen the answer key to some universal examination and had found it remarkably unexciting. I remember her suddenly raising her voice. It was hoarse and raspy, like she had spent a lifetime yelling. ‘Of all the possibilities. Why this?’ Then she grew quiet, fully silent for a while. I could hear the ship humming along below as she slowly gathered her thoughts. When she spoke again, it was with trepidation. She explained that there had to be something beneath the dust. Something else. The ALICE chimed in there, adding that their equations had been balanced, that knowing this side of the black hole’s face had greatly simplified the mathematics. There were no further mysterious variables or imaginary numbers needed to explain the physical simplicities of this space. The woman grew agitated as the ALICE spoke, and she began yelling over the ship. She was insistent on some menace beneath the sinking sea, and she would not allow the ALICE to suggest that the math needed to support her fantasies. She said the depths of our unknowing are unfathomably endless. I eventually left. With them shouting over each other, it was impossible to get a word in. Besides, they didn’t seem interested in my input.”

Evan would then hand Adam a pink velvet high heel, just one without a partner, an exotic sort of souvenir to prove that his words weren’t simply air, that the physicist did in fact lay about somewhere out there, half-coherent in a lonely lifeboat. The thought of such a mind going to mush saddened Adam, a slow, maddening decay of the lines between a mind, its dreamings, and the world beyond them.
“I found someone,” Evan would say, another thoughtful tangent in Adam’s mind practically tripping over the story of the physicist’s findings.

“Tell me everything,” Adam would respond, whispering the words alone on a trail of dust in a world where Evan would likely never say such a thing, starting before the thought of Evan had even finished. The dust would collect in dunes around them, seated beside each other at the pod door.

“I was walking along, as usual, when in the corner of my ear I heard what sounded like laughter off in the distance. I didn’t see anything at first but then I heard it again - someone shouting and cackling somewhere far away. I broke from my path and started off toward the sound’s apparent source. I soon saw it - a pod’s dome - growing on the horizon. The dust was restless today, but I made pretty good speed traversing the distance between me and the pod’s talkative occupants. As I grew closer though, I began to realize that it was only one person speaking, though they easily filled the space of a full dialogue and then some. Through the closed door I could hear them say something, sharp and punchy, then retort at themself, smooth and songful. They would erupt into laughs that echoed inside the pod and resonated into the ship’s hum. At first I was unsure if I ought to disturb them. I was frankly unsure I could keep up with this person in conversation. But then I thought that I couldn’t return empty-handed, without even having seen this person’s face. So I knocked, loud enough to make sure they heard. The ship became suddenly quiet, aside from the humming that held it aloft. The door opened and, well Adam, you wouldn’t have believed it. This woman, she was so short and rotund. She practically croaked when she saw me. I followed her in as she waddled to her table. I was confused. She looked so, well, well-fed. It was bizarre, especially with how lean you and I run. When I asked how she had remained so full in a place without food, she roared with laughter. ‘In this place we can eat our words.’ She explained it like that, like that explained everything. She clearly saw what must have been a dumb-founded look on my face, because without another word she launched right into a rolling litany of spoken literature. It seemed that her mind was a veritable library, and she was checking out every book in immediate succession. I was at first still confused, though I quickly settled in simply to listen to the fantastic prose she spouted, tales of fever dreams and sweltering midnights, tiny people toppling towering giants, dark birds whispering dark words in protagonist’s ears. These stories were unlike anything I had heard before. The details felt familiar and yet entirely new. She had just fallen into a patterned iambic rhythm, something organic and organized, when the words started to appear. I’m telling you, Adam. It was unlike anything I’ve ever even dreamed. Her words appeared there on the table, right in front of us. They were gray and blocky, and they faded into existence out of the air. Once one started, the rest followed quickly. Soon they were piling on top of each other. Each word held together like an interlocking set. They were stacking and falling, tumbling over each other. The mountain grew to the point that I had to stand to see her again, as its peak reached up to block her gaze. She had kept her eyes trained on me this whole time. Her poetry pivoted to explain what was happening, that the words had all been edible. Everything she had tried at least. And different flavors in every bite with no apparent connection between the word’s meaning and what it evoked on the tongue. Nevertheless, she had eaten to fulfillment since arriving. She even had to throw out her leftovers, or else she would have run out of room. It seems that they never went stale, and she and her ALICE had mistakenly taken to very verbose storytelling early on. ALICE had since been relegated to silence to prevent unnecessary accumulation of the junk. I saw what she meant. The other levels of the ship were filled to brimming with words upon words. Prepositions piled on pronouns.”

Evan would then hand Adam a word: ENOUGH. It would be firm and colorless in his hand, evocative of some kind of root vegetable, a turnip perhaps but more elongated. He would lift the word to his mouth and take a bold, crisp bite. The flavor would be like licking bacon after a cigarette has been freshly lit nearby, or perhaps it would be a tart flavor like mascarpone with a lilt of fig and cracked pepper on the back end.
Adam’s mouth was watering desperately, foolishly, drooling over near forgotten flavors. His mind was falling prey to the pains of hunger, his daydreaming pulled toward flights of fancy.
“I thought I had found someone.” The novelty in Evan’s imagined words pulled Adam’s attention inward once again. He was intrigued by this deviation from his expectation.
Adam would kneel at Evan’s feet, helping to undo the fasteners on the heavy boots. Dust would spill from hidden nooks and niches, let loose by Adam’s busy hands. “Tell me everything,” he would say.

“It was shockingly quiet on the sea this time, and when I stopped to reorient, I heard the hum. Just like our ship, the way it goes - thrum thrum thrum - with that pulsing beat. So I started following the sound. I’d stop for a moment, quiet my mind as the dust settled, then I would hear it again - thrum thrum thrum - just like that. I wandered that way for quite a while, and ALICE’s beat in the eyepiece grew fainter the further I went. I moved without their beacon for a while before I grew too nervous. The thought of going untethered on the sea was just too immense, Adam. It was when I was turning back that I saw it - closer than I had anticipated. So close that I was shocked to have missed it. Immediately I could see that the pod door was open. It felt garish the way the ship was so exposed. From where I stood, far off, the pod looked like an eyeball, the open door a tightened pupil. It seemed to watch as I approached. At first I tried calling into the ship, but there was no answer, only the ship’s thrum thrum thrum. I called a few times, but after the absence seemed assured, I felt bold enough to enter. The interior was covered in dust. It had started to make its way in through the open door. There were dunes all over the room, and the lower level appeared nearly full. It reminded me of our lower garden, actually. What it might look like if we keep layering on top of the old attempts, the ones that haven’t worked yet. I eventually climbed the ladder, looking for anything of substance, but even the floor above was bare. There were at most shallow puddles of dust - whatever had blown in and up by happenstance. But otherwise, it was all empty. I was prepared to give up and go when the ALICE spoke to me. I had completely forgotten about them, but then the thrum thrum thrum should have tipped me off. As I spoke with the ALICE, I learned that a man had been aboard the ship once, a man who had a primal craving for an audience, and who had found nothing of the sort in this quiet place. The ALICE had attempted to play audio bites of applause and project stunned faces in dark auditorium seats on the screens around him, but nothing sufficed. He had cleared the ship, insisting that he have the whole stage for himself, throwing it all into the dust. In the end, he had shared one final utterance, his moment of profound clarity, before marching into the distant dust himself.”

Adam would wait in fascinated silence, watching Evan’s eyes carefully, seeking the words before they could be spoken. Evan’s face would turn in a look of deep confusion. “Ya la bulla.” He would say it like that - flat and clear - a beat of hope in his eyes revealing the anticipation that Adam could explain the meaning in this foreign tongue. “Ya la bulla.” He would repeat it, more sternly this time. “What does it mean?”
Adam would sigh and rise to his feet, brushing away the dust on his trousers. “It means nothing.”

Being reminded of the orator’s profound gibberish shook Adam from the daydream, a muttering with no meaning that gummed his grinding mental gears. He pulled his knees higher with each step, pressing down loosely packed dust.
“I found someone.” Evan’s face in Adam’s mind’s eye was flush, pink with a thrill the boy had never known.
Adam would smell the faint perfume of champagne on Evan’s breath as he drew near. He would brush the dusting of gray from Evan’s shoulders, helping as he shed the heavy suit. “Tell me everything.”

“I was nearly back to our pod. It was a tough bungee this time. The dust was loose - practically fighting me the whole way. I was more than ready to be back here and on solid footing again. The suit had picked up a lot of dust, and I was tired from the extra weight. But then I saw it – a second pod in the distance beyond our own. At first, I thought my eyes were deceiving me, but no matter how hard I blinked, the round shape stayed. It was tough, but I kept going. I went right past our pod and made a direct line for it. As I grew closer, I was shocked to see movement. The door of the pod opened and out stepped a woman. She felt somehow magnificent despite her stature. She ran to me, as if she knew who I was. Before I could say anything, she was embracing me. She squeezed her arms around my stiff torso. My face was buried in her wild mane. We stayed like that for a while. I was at a loss for words, for reason. I realized she was whispering something, and I listened more closely to the mumbling against my chest. ‘You came,’ she was saying, over and over. ‘You came. You came.’ Eventually I had to take her inside of the pod. We had sunk almost to our knees. We sat in the entry for an eternity - just us, and the dust and the silence. It was right when she seemed ready to speak again that the ALICE  made themself known. They explained nearly everything. The pod had once had two occupants - a man and this woman. The first was a king, the ALICE told me, though his whereabouts were unknown. The second, this woman, was a queen. I wondered what it meant to be royalty on the dust sea. I remember watching her eyes shifting as the ALICE laid bare all that they knew. I inquired about the king. I wanted to know what had happened, why she had been left so alone. The ALICE grew silent on the matter. The queen only said, ‘He’s gone.’ It was clear they did not want to speak on it further, and she rose to her feet. She took my hand and led me to a modest bed across the room. She laid me down, and her hands went to work on my body. I was taken aback, so quickly becoming the clay in this sculptor’s hands. But it felt remarkably good, and I fell shortly into blissful comfort. She kneaded me like dough. Her hands were strong, stronger than I had anticipated. They sought out my tensions, felt for knots and stones carried beneath my skin. And then they pushed. Pressed. Kneaded. She kneaded me, and I was transported. I flew off to faraway places, worlds of blurred light and letters, cinder and steel, swelling symphonies that silenced every small thought in my wandering mind. Then it was her tongue that was wandering. She loved the taste of my sweat. Her searching, her seeking, drew my mind back there again. It called me back from forgotten chapters of memory. It rang some bell in me, set off a low tone that resonated right down into my loins. The note swelled up inside of me, and I was singing opera, tenor moans punctuated by heady sighs. My diaphragm writhed, arched, and pressed us closer. We were an orchestra packed in the pit. The song was quickening its pace. Our fingers flew to make melodies that could keep in time. Her lips found my neck, and my skin was suddenly buzzing. My heart fluttered madly. I was a hummingbird. My tongue tasted her like a fresh bloom. Her sweet nectar made my mouth water with delight. She spit with force into her hand – a sudden, staccato punctuation against our smooth tones. She slid her slick palm down and grabbed hold of my firm form.”

Adam lost his footing, falling to his hands and knees in the gray sludge. He had fallen deep into the wandering of his own mind and lost track of his whereabouts. He fought to pull himself free of the binding hold of the tenuous shadow of earth. The beacon in his eyepiece had grown faint, inconsistent, and as he rose to his feet, he turned wildly, frantically searching for strength in ALICE's signal. Managing to realign himself with their position, Adam fell into a brisk near trot directly back. He was increasingly fearful about traversing the dust expanse. The way that his wandering in this world set his mind adrift left him worried that he might wake to find himself buried too deep to escape, too far gone to resurface. His heart was banging manically in his chest, a methodical, mechanical, undeniable pulse that threatened to break free. He felt lightheaded, his eyes crossing paths for a moment.
“I found someone.” Evan's voice arose unexpectedly. Adam fought to focus on his steps. His balance felt at best dubious.

Suddenly Adam was being pummeled. “How dare you?” the woman's voice came out of nowhere, unfamiliar against the typical trio of the pod. Adam in his mind turned to look up from the microscope in time for another volley of mad slaps and halfhearted fists. Adam threw his arms up to block her, spying the near forgotten face of the doula. Across the room, Evan stood sullen near the pod door.
“She was alone in her pod and insisted on returning with me.”

Even as he spoke, the doula continued beating at Adam, her fists coming down hard. He imagined the bruises he would certainly develop, a patchwork of bleeding blue and black across his arms. “How dare you?” she cried again. Adam felt lost. This was far from the reunion he had envisioned. Evan crossed his arms in the shadows.
“All of her kids are gone.”

The doula’s face was awash in tears that glistened even in the low light. Adam recalled the sea of faces that had so often enveloped the doula, a herd of dark-haired children tugging at her hands, braying for their mother's attention. He wondered what had come of them all. Had they grown distant, crammed together in the confines of their pod? Perhaps their collective grief had fomented sibling rivalries into all out brawls, fights that tumbled into the dust, leaving wounds too deep to heal. How many of the doula’s children had walked off alone to the horizon out of spite or shame, blinded by hormonal fury and the salt of familiar tears? Adam could see the doula standing at the threshold of her pod, watching anxiously for the silhouette of a child who'd changed their mind, who'd seen the light before dying in the dim distance, who'd spared her a life drowning in sorrow. Perhaps she would wonder if there had been a flaw in her methodologies of motherhood. Evan was crying now, gray lines curving through the fine dust caked on his face.
“She said you carried her things. The materials she needed.”

Her hands beat relentlessly at Adam. He tried to grab a hold, to take her arms and stop her, but she moved too quickly in the faint light of the room in his mind. “How dare you?” she repeated. Adam's eyes grew wide, realizing how cavalier he'd been with the doula’s ova, how certain he'd been that he was alone enough to take them as his own for experimentation. He tried to remember how many of the original stash remained - one? Two? Had he even retained enough of the ochre powder to maintain another gestation? He feared there would be no appeasing this forlorn mother, who so desperately continued to thrash at his mind.

Adam felt the onslaught of punches quickening, growing in their fury. Across the room Evan's face was buried in his hands, his shoulders heaving as he sobbed wildly. “How dare you? How dare you?” Her hands found every opening. Fingers flicked at his eyes. She nipped at his skin like a weak and wild animal famished for a taste of blood. At last she found an avenue past his frantically swinging arms and grabbed his throat. Her hands were strong, and they crushed his windpipe easily. Adam's eyes popped as his world grew dark at the edges, a shrinking circle haloing the doula’s unforgiving face. Adam tried to turn, to catch Evan’s eye before the life drained out of him, but her hold kept him locked on her, the living reminder of his shame. He opened his mouth to speak but made only a cracking sputter. The world went to static, then bleeding yellow like fresh yolk, then black.
Adam woke with a start on his cot, the taste of sulfur fresh in his mouth. He sat up, his hands finding his throat, feeling again the grip of the raging doula.
“I found you,” Evan said. Adam turned to find him by the bed, a mug in each hand. He offered one, though Adam turned it away. His heart was already pounding noticeably in his chest.

Evan explained that they had ALICE to thank. When Adam's location had stalled on the way back, ALICE had brought it to Evan's attention. “I found you up to your ears in the dust.” Adam fell back on the wall by the cot and closed his eyes. Though Evan had returned to work at the microscope, Adam could still feel himself being watched in the room. For the moment, Adam defied sleep, teetering on the edge as he considered the security of his position.

chapters.title: LEAVING THE NEST
paragraphs: “Keep going.”

Adam beckoned from his seat on the cot. Already Evan had set a picturesque scene: a sea of greens and yellows that swayed into the caress of a passing breeze, years marked by the sun’s turning arcs across a vast blue sky, the creak of boards beneath a rocking chair positioned like a throne on a porch. The story had immediately taken Adam somewhere familiar, his home on another world, in another life. He had pictured himself in the comfortable seat, looking out over his realm, feeling as the morning sun grew increasingly bold on his skin. He could smell the dew on the grass, the earthen bite of his mug’s fresh brew held beneath his nose. With his eyes closed, he could just feel the promise of the day ahead. For now, this was where he wanted to stay. He encouraged Evan once again.
“Keep going.”
Across the dim room, Evan was studying something with the microscope. Without lifting his eyes, he continued.

“It was an unusually hot day in autumn when the weight of the old pantry door quite unexpectedly caused its own hinges to buckle and break. The swollen slab sat staunchly in place, though I struggled for weeks to remove it. The door’s edges clutched at the adjoining frame so tightly there was no room to budge nor moment to breathe. I considered taking an axe to the damn thing - how satisfying it would have been to swing that sharp slick iron and splinter the stubborn shit! – but its face was something to be admired. An ornate fresco was puckered among the grooves and crags of the facade, the stunningly intricate carvings of some family carpenter long since passed. Miniature scenes stretched between the knots and whorls – far-off eagles frozen in flight, rugged frontiersman poised above a pristine vista of rolling hills and towering pine, a stalwart ship riding an insatiable storm. How could I live with destroying such beauty? By winter it was deemed a lost cause. Try as I might, I negotiated not one inch from the obstinate piece of oak. It came to be a brick in the wall rather than a door the way that it stood so cemented and steadfast. With each new day, I examined each detail of its sublime scenery – the number of boxcars snaking through a rocky crevasse, the astral trails of fireworks above a starlit cityscape, the forlorn face of a stately figurehead mounted on a ship’s bow. And yet I came to hear the scratches of vermin claws dancing inside the sealed chamber. A cacophony of scuffs and scrapes littered the nights as the rats reveled in this misfortune, screeching as they squabbled over sleeves of saltines. The sounds of their ceaseless savagery echoed through the floorboards of the house and left my nights sleepless and strained. All the while those mindless beasts paid no heed to the festering piles of filth at their feet. Come spring, the whole house stunk of shit and rot, but by then I had grown too accustomed to the stench to even realize. Those noxious fumes had anchored in my lungs. Their poisons pounded like mortars in my heart and weakened my weary mind. My eyes grew swollen and red, agitated from a relentless stream of gray tears that trickled down my face. My shallow bouts of sleep became riddled with fitful dreams of drowning, and I would sometimes half wake to find myself out of bed and standing at the pantry door. In the darkness my hands would press their way along that extravagant tapestry trapped in time and timber – a column of smoke rising above a regal steamboat, a glacier cascading between snow-capped peaks, waves caressing a moonlit shore -  and my fingertips, splintered and bloody, tapped in time with the frantic footsteps behind the door. Lazily I would hum some half-forgotten anthem until the grace of slumber took me once again like a castaway swallowed by a salty sea. One crisp summer night I dreamt of sea foam on my skin and sun on my face. With a start I woke to inferno - roaring masts of smoke tossing and tangoing in the flickering glow of hungry flames. They licked the walls that held the fulcrum of the hellish blaze: the pantry door. A candle lay prone on the floor, knocked over by reckless rats or a careless sleepwalker, I couldn’t say. I strained my eyes against the vicious heat to see the door’s flamboyant façade once more, to see that life held fast in a moment, but I could make out little more than a fierce and blinding light. Gasping, I turned and fumbled out of the house, falling to my knees, my bloodied fingers grasping at cool earth, as behind me the walls moaned, lamenting the kisses of flame. I could hear the oak slab pop and crack in the hellish halls of my forsaken home, but it wasn’t until daybreak when the pantry door finally gave way. The sun had not yet seen fit to peek over the horizon, and already it was an unusually hot morning, when with a momentous crash and a sickening belch, a life crumbled to ash in the twilit breaths of a new day’s dawn.”

Up to this point, Adam had held his eyes closed to better picture the unfolding story, but feeling the heavy heat of the collapsing pyre brought him back to attention in the room. Evan had gone silent, perhaps anticipating Adam had grown bored with the meandering tale. They waited there in the quiet, Adam in the dark behind his lids, anticipating Evan’s next words.

When the silence grew stale, Adam opened his eyes. He was no longer sitting on the cot, but rather at the microscope. The binocular lenses stood resolutely before his face, flicks of movement visible down through the eyepieces. He didn't remember what had brought him here.
