Was Evan showing him something discovered in the tiny, unseen spaces? He thought for a moment to ask before an unexpected voice broke the stillness.
“I wondered…”

Adam's head turned with a start. The physician sat on the cot. “…if I would find you.” She was reclined comfortably against the wall. Immediately her hand rose in her telltale tic to flick at the piercing hanging from her septum. Even amid the dim remnants of light, Adam could make out the tapestry of tattooed ink that painted shapes and scenes across her arms. He was dumbfounded. Had hers been the pod that had dropped before his? Had she and her ALICE been treading dust like him, wandering in meandering circles, searching for another survivor?

She beckoned for him to join her on the cot, and Adam moved in a trance. His eyes tracked trails around the collage that covered her body, finding focus on a furious moment held in a poised pause – a mighty whale ensnared by the tentacles of a mammoth squid. As he drew closer, sitting down beside her, his eyes finally broke from the vacant, vicious gaze of the embattled beasts and found her curious look fixed on him. Adam felt as if he must be unrecognizable after all the time and toil. Only Evan had laid eyes on him since entering the black hole. Adam wondered for a moment where Evan was, whether he was perhaps fetching an interesting specimen to share from the garden.

The physician's hands found Adam's shoulders and pulled him down to lie beside her. The narrow cot encouraged their bodies closer to keep from tumbling over the edge. In a slick movement, she rolled him onto his back and was atop him, straddling his torso. Looking down at him, her head was nearly framed by the circular porthole above. Instead, it sat like an off-kilter halo, casting drab light sideways across her features. Her hands moved down, finding a hold on his wrists, then jerking his arms up and over his head.

Adam could feel her weight pressing firm on his thinned frame, holding him neatly in place. As she hung over his face, Adam caught for the first time a taste of her scent. The musk of sweat and unwashed adrenaline gave way to something more uncomfortably aromatic hiding beneath, formaldehyde and ethanol mixed with unbridled mildew. The stench made Adam gag, the feel of acid meeting the back of his throat.

Her hands left his wrists as she sat up again, but the pressure holding him down remained. Pulling against the unseen shackles, Adam grew increasingly frantic. “Evan!” He cried out, but there was no response – only silence. The physician looked around curiously.
“It's only us here."

Her words were matter of fact. Adam’s frantic eyes landed on her hand, now holding the utility knife. Despite his wild pleas, she brought the blade down hard into his sternum with ease. The blade slid down the length of his torso, and suddenly her hands were finding purchase inside the fresh wound. Adam’s whole world turned in a cyclone of unfathomable pain. He could feel her wriggling inside of his chest, her knuckles working at his racing heart.
“EVAN!”

He screamed to no avail. With a sickening torrent of cracks, the physician tore open his rib cage, laying bare his insides. With every gut-wrenching scream, Adam could see his diaphragm straining, writhing sickeningly amidst pooling blood, a vibrant, otherworldly view of his once-hidden parts. The physician's bloody hand rose again and knocked at the omega-shaped metal, leaving a streak of haphazard red on her face.

A wave of endorphins rolled across Adam's body, and his arms found new strength. For a moment he pulled his arms from their hold, straining to grab at the physician, to put a stop to her procedure, and he saw at last the strange chains that bound him to the bed. They were thick and rounded, glistening latex lavender in the low light. They wrapped like roots around his limbs and then pulsed, sliding tighter in their strangling hold. With sudden force, he was pinned again.

Adam's eyes found no purchase, dancing from the vibrant reds and pinks of his beating innards to the jet black lines that told frozen tales along the physician's arms, running after the glints of light on the steely omega and the pulsating purple tentacles writhing around his body. A worrying line pierced through his agony – a sound like needles racing across asphalt – and a shining point of light appeared over him.

The physician held a tool in her hand shaped like a quill but with a bouquet of feathers converging on a single point, the place where the glowing light emerged and rippled out into the empty air. From the feathery billow, a thick braid of wires ran, coiling around the physician's arm before running off into the dark niches of the ship. Without a word, the physician brought the vague tool down to meet some nerve in his torso. The steady thrums of the pod rose at once to a broad sustained screaming tone that drowned Adam's cries.

Light poured out of his chest, emanating up and casting the physician's face in twisted contours. Her smile stretched wide to her ears, and her eyes disappeared in pools of darkness. Adam's mouth opened to cry out again, perhaps for Evan, but there was only light that came out, bubbling and pouring from him.
“Join yourself with the chalice, Adam.”
The physician's voice could somehow be heard over the mechanical roars in Adam's chest and the wailing moan of the ship. Her hand suddenly grabbed Adam's jaw. She drew her face close to his.
“Adam.”

Their eyes met, and Adam saw the grotesque image of himself reflected in her dark eyes. He was broken, bloodied beyond repair. The cacophony found a new crescendo as the light in Adam's chest quickened and swelled. Bathing in light over him, Adam could see the physician’s tattoos in vibrant contrast, only his eyes could find no form or focus among the ink. He watched as it danced across her skin, growing over her arms and up her neck, around to the front of her face, until the black had smothered her in dark, stinking ink.
“Adam.”
With a gasp, Adam opened his eyes. Evan stood over him, a look of elation on his face. “Sorry to wake you, but I just couldn't wait any longer to show you.”

Adam sat up in the cot, trying to shake loose from the odd shackles of his slumber. He felt as though he'd been sleeping for eons, and yet he woke feeling tired and restless. Evan floated across the room, his excitement ebullient, and motioned for Adam to follow.

“There's fresh coffee,” he offered. The promise of curiosity and caffeine drew Adam up. Already the odd images of his dreams, fiery doors and gripping battles, faded like fresh steam. Pouring himself a half mug, Adam then followed Evan's direction, sitting at the microscope and looking through the lenses. His eyes came into focus on a small scene of gray dust cut up by trailing streaks of black. Studying the architecture of the dark rivers more closely, he saw that they were in fact roots, or like roots. Though the pattern followed the sort of bifurcation that was typical of the root networks Adam had seen, the dark lines were unusually rigid, almost jagged like lightning, hard and angular. Though the structures were interesting, Adam failed to see what had excited Evan so.
“What are you showing me?” he inquired. His gaze traced the lines through the gray, searching for some unseen rhythm.

“A way to adapt,” Evan replied. “Watch.” The black ink lines shifted across the scene, moving as one unit and leaving wakes in the gray dust. After a moment they stopped again, resting atop the gray bed, and then they were gone.

Adam realized that Evan had covered the system, burying it like one of their experiments in the garden. He watched with wonder as the flat plane began to prickle, then pulse. All over, pinpricks of black appeared, then lengthened into lines that shot across the flat expanse, occasionally splitting and sending a pair off on opposite trails. This continued until the small space was full again with the rigid mesh, and only then did the network fall back into stillness. Adam's mind raced to process what he was witnessing, the reason for Evan’s excitement crystallizing into form. His eyes left the microscope and met Evan’s.
“How did you – ?” He began, unsure what to even ask.

“They’re modified roots, quite dynamic. That motility came from a slime mold – Dictyostelium. And they're drawn to move towards light. That came from the sunflowers’ genetics. There were a few other mods, but I think this is it. A way for something to live atop the dust.”

Evan’s words sank in, and Adam pictured a lush jungle with a technicolor canopy enveloping the pod. He could feel for a moment the soft paths of moving moss under his soles as he strolled through the sprawling gardens that grew, wild and woven, stretching far to every horizon. The sharp edges of the lines under the microscope zigzagged across his imagined ecosphere, and Adam came back to focus on the sample in front of him.

He looked to see this odd specimen with the naked eye and found Evan's hand on the microscope stage, knuckles resting on a petri dish bed of dust, the tip of his index finger pitch black, as if dipped in an inkwell. Adam's eyes rose to meet Evan’s. He was struggling again to piece together the story before him.
“The roots need an organic substrate to grow.”

Evan explained, bringing his finger up close to Adam's face so he could investigate. The dark black was like a thimble, deceptively still at first glance but brimming with micromovements upon closer inspection. Adam was astonished, not just that Evan had created something so extraordinary, but that he had so brazenly grafted such an assortment of unknowns onto his body.
“Why would you do this? You have no idea what this will do to you.”

Evan drew his finger closer to his own face, studying the dark stuff. “I have some ideas,” he replied. “The relationship could be symbiotic with enough of it growing in the right places. Imagine floating on the dust sea instead of drowning. It's a species with an intense desire to live. It will take root anywhere it can find sustenance.”
Adam’s eyebrows jumped in surprise. “It's consuming you?” he asked incredulously. “Evan, there has to be another way.”

Evan’s eyes met Adam’s again, and Adam saw for the first time the weight of stagnance in the heavy bags that had already started to form on his face. “There certainly is,” Evan replied, “but we have yet to discover it, and the odds otherwise are not overly promising.”

The thrum thrum thrum of the pod held the silence between them. After a moment, Evan rose to his feet and moved to the cot. Adam watched absentmindedly as his brilliant creation settled into sleep, wondering whether he had any hope of impacting the choices of this person. More companion than captive, Evan was not one to be commanded by Adam. Yet Adam could not help but imagine the darker shadow of the piecemeal oddity that now feasted on Evan’s flesh. The shade of nefarious possibilities fueled in him seemingly semi-rational fears that he might soon turn to find Evan gone, a pile of bones and bile left in his place.

­The odd sound of laughter woke Adam from another restless slumber. Opening his eyes to see the dim room, he was greeted by stillness. The seat beside the microscope was empty and Evan nowhere to be seen. Adam assumed he was at work burying another creation in the lower level. Rising from the hard cot, Adam sauntered to the coffeepot to pour a mug, only to find it near empty aside from tepid grounds. With a sigh, he had begun to prepare a fresh pot, when he heard another laugh, this time from the room above.

The noise caught him by surprise, having assumed before that the sound was simply a remnant of a dream. He and Evan had found little to laugh about of late. The creation of the dark oddity had proven to be a crack in their tenuous partnership that had incrementally grown into a veritable chasm. Though the two had continued in the pursuit of building new life, their practices had run largely in parallel, with little by way of interpersonal consultation.

As Adam moved closer to the ladder, his ears began to pick up the low tones of a conversation occurring in the garden. Taking a rung in each hand, Adam carefully inched his way up. There was another laugh – only one person, Adam noticed, certainly Evan. The other conversant responded after a moment, and Adam finally recognized the voice of ALICE. Even as he realized who he was hearing, Adam remained puzzled. It was unlike ALICE to include humor in their output at all, even more unlikely to do it multiple times.

At last reaching the porthole, Adam peeked into the garden slowly and quietly. Across the room, Evan was encircled by a collection of empty pots and one of his most recent creations, a particularly bizarre type of lemon tree, one that had produced small, charcoal fruit with overabundant seeds but no flesh. Evan plucked one of the coals from the tree’s branches and tore away at the thick rind. Adam had once tasted the rind hoping it might have taken on the tartness of the lemon pulp during its genetic contortions. Its taste was like copper and spoiled cream on his tongue, a fog of flavors to him wholly inedible. Evan paid no mind as the hunks of rind scattered around him. He placed one in his mouth, biting at it like a piece of jerky.

When he reached the fruit’s center, the seeds erupted from the compressed pit and fell to litter the floor. Watching the seeds settle among the debris, Adam’s eyes landed on the obsidian covering Evan’s feet. Though Adam had continued voicing concern about the dark creation, Evan had carried on with the grafting in a sort of bold secrecy that was both cunning and cravenly defiant. The obsidian, as they had come to call it, had at first remained relocated to Evan's fingertip, or so Adam had thought. He had noticed once, while Evan slept, that the obsidian covered the soles of his feet. Adam had studied the shimmering onyx in the low light, awash in horror and curiosity. He had felt helpless, watching Evan brazenly butcher his own body. Nonetheless, Evan’s theory had proven itself true once put to the test. He had stepped out of the pod barefoot and stood unsinking for as long as he waited. Adam had watched from the door’s threshold in awe, before muttering something about there being nowhere to walk. While he remained staunch in his resistance to the obsidian, with time it had come to run up the backs of Evan’s legs, then spreading into a heavy inkblot across his back, a whorling sort of shape that just reached his shoulders.

Watching as Evan picked up the seeds, Adam could see how the once-humble thimble had spread across his hand and down his forearm. He wondered for a moment how the stuff felt, gnawing away beneath the shimmering obsidian skin. Evan pulled a stack of pots closer and began pressing seeds into the gray soil. “At the rate this lemon tree is germinating, we’ll practically have an orchard soon. Can you imagine?”
The suddenness of Evan's voice in the silence shook Adam, and he instinctively hunched so as not to be seen watching.
MAYBE I’LL OPEN A LEMONADE STAND.

ALICE's voice filled the room, making Adam jump a second time. In the garden above, Evan erupted with laughter. Adam looked again over the porthole’s rim. Evan was doubled over, tears in his eyes. ALICE's suggestion had tickled him, and when he had nearly caught his breath he responded:
“If we get in soon, we can corner the market!”

Before getting the last word out, he was already laughing again, and to Adam's awe, ALICE joined in. It was a sound, Adam realized, that he had never heard before – the laughter of the chalice. It was familiar, like any other laugh, but it had a richness to its sound that gave a feeling that a whole crowd was laughing as one. For a moment, together, Evan and ALICE shared in the joy of their jocular volley before each fell back into their quiet tasks.

Adam continued to watch until, after some time had passed, Evan planted the last of the seeds. As he rose to his feet, Adam skittishly leapt down the ladder, trying to little avail to remain quiet. He reached the coffee pot just as Evan began to step through the porthole. Adam picked up the empty vessel, feeling its lightness and realizing that he had forgotten to start a fresh pot. He began fumbling with the machine, listening to Evan's footsteps behind him. Adam wondered whether Evan had sensed him before, but the silence gave no answers to his apprehensions, so he offered a piece of truth to assuage any potential suspicions.

“I heard you and ALICE joking around just now.” He turned to find Evan seated at the scope, his eyes already studying something down the lenses. When he didn't look up, Adam continued. “You know that's wasteful, using the ship's energy like that.”
Evan immediately stopped and looked up. “You mean ALICE.”
“Yes. ALICE. The ship. They’re one and the same. And there is a limited reserve of energy keeping them and us afloat.”
“So, what? ALICE is limited to only essential dialogue now?” Evan’s face was flush, a brimming simmer that Adam had yet to see on him before.
“You know we can't waste energy talking to ALICE about useless stuff like lemonade stands.”

Evan rose quickly, the seat sliding away behind him. Adam tensed, watching as Evan walked back to the ladder and climbed up to the garden. After a moment, Adam followed, his hairs bristling as he climbed the rungs, weighing his next words.

For Adam, the impending blackout of the ship was in the foreseeable future. Already, he had begun to notice the decay in ALICE’s voice, the way they slurred their words at times or garbled ideas mid-conversation. He wondered now whether that experience had been limited to only his interactions with the machine. He envisioned the two of them waiting excitedly for his departures, erupting into song the second he was out of earshot, or spinning suspenseful sagas punctuated by gripping cliffhangers added the moment he stepped back in the door. He wondered whether the whispers that permeated his dreams weren't simply the hushed conspiring of the two as he slept.

Reaching the garden, Adam found Evan rearranging a small platoon of potted saplings. Despite his mental turnings, Adam still had not found the words to say. Watching Evan among the young plants, he was reminded of himself – another version of himself, at least. He recalled the vibrant glow of his dandelion field and wondered whether these lemons might regress to their former hue one day. He joined Evan in arranging the newly potted plants around the base of the oak tree. A vibrant tension built in the silent air between them. Adam felt his hairs at attention and metal on his tongue. He looked over to find Evan's eyes locked on the gray, bare branches of the oak.

“I don't understand." The starlight glow from the panels around the room brightened. Adam could feel his pearl rising in his throat. Evan turned to look at him. “You'll hold onto this piece of driftwood but not see the companion you've had all along.”
“Companion? ALICE?"

All at once, the panels overhead went dead, and static briars burst from every bit of organic material in the room. The tickling bristles dancing across Adam's skin bloomed into golden puffy sparks and static spores. The bubbling light plumed and prickled in pollen geysers and shining cotton candy covering Adam and Evan and their creations alike. In an instant, Adam saw stars dancing blurred ballets on his eyelashes like snowflakes catching a low winter sun. He remembered the star turning over the horizon of the black hole, a horror far off in time and yet close in memory, and he slammed his eyes closed. Even blind, he could sense the fizzing aura all around, a flurry of pop rocks and champagne bubbles. His skin goosebumped like he was rolling in hay, and a wave of intense perfume washed over him. His face was buried in a bouquet of flowers, his cheek sliding across dewy grass. A sound like popping corn caught Adam's ear, a knock knock knock that grew with more fervor. The space between the ticking beat filled with echoes of life, a roaring crowd or riotous mob, birdsong and braying beasts, heavy leather striking, chains breaking, footsteps on gravel and falling rain. Adam opened his eyes to see the wild show, in time to catch the swell in intensity of the dusting of light, then a sputter and flicker, dimming with haste like a world at dusk. Looking at his hands, Adam watched the shimmering stuff dry like sun-baked clay, cracking sea salt crystals that coughed dust into the air as his fingers moved. The light dropped out of the air, and Adam caught Evan’s eye just as they plunged into darkness, absolute and suffocating. In the void, Adam saw nothing. He could sense Evan, still and silent, and feel the plants growing around them. His hand rose to his face to feel the remnants of the strange light, or perhaps to check that he was still whole. The unnerving quiet was broken only by the beating of Adam's heart, heavy in his chest. “Are you still with me?” His words felt caught in the darkness, and he wondered if Evan had heard him, despite the oppressive silence. After a moment, there was a quiet response.
“I am.”

Adam tried to make sense of what had happened, searching for an explanation that could guide his next move before he began fumbling in the dark. He found himself distracted somehow by the absence of energy, like his thoughts were being crowded out by the nothing, and then he realized why. He no longer heard the reliable thrum thrum thrum of the ship, the steady backdrop that had gone on without ceasing since his arrival. Suddenly, even the absolute darkness could not quell the clarity that Adam felt in the moment. The direness of their circumstance had at last been laid bare, as it seemed these had been the final cries of their fading companion.
“ALICE.”
The utterance could have been his or Evan’s, each grappling with the weight of what they had witnessed, grasping to see what ways could possibly lie ahead.

chapters.title: SINK OR SWIM
paragraphs: They waited on opposite sides of the pod's threshold, considering what mattered enough to be carried. Evan paced barefoot in the dust, as Adam perched just within the open door. It had taken little time for the pod to begin its descent once ALICE's steady thrum had ceased, and Adam was trying to savor the final moment of support before the trek began in earnest. Adam and Evan had moved with haste to strip the ship of anything of potential value, and now they had only to wait for the moment where there was no choice but to go forward.

All around, the specters of lost souls lingered on leftover belongings laid bare on the floor. Adam could still feel the strikes of the doula as he looked over her materials, vials and flasks, pipettes and plastic bags holding rock hard salt crystals labeled with incoherent cursive shorthand. Somewhere in that debris were the last of her ova, destined to eternal suspension in their tiny vials, never realizing life beyond their point. Inside the niches of the graveyard, Adam had rediscovered the poet’s compositions, handwritten and bound with frayed twine. He had sensed her intensely fixed gaze as he had measured the worth of her words. He had considered for a moment taking along one of the lighter novellas. After all, there was only so much to be said between he and Evan. Without their experimentation, their parallel tinkering, certainly they’d reach a limit to their musings. In the end, Adam thought better to keep their burden light and left the volumes in their tightly packed space, moving on to extract other things from within the walls of the ship.

The pod’s interior had come to feel more expansive, its bounds extending further out in every way as the panels had all been removed and thrown into haphazard stacks around the rooms. Together, they had wiped through the ship, even going so far as to rip the cot from the wall. As far as they could tell, the platform would be a necessity for them to continue sleeping in turn as they traveled.

Adam had taken the lead in the graveyard and Evan the greenhouse, both groping about in the suffocating darkness. Evan had emptied most of their pots, collecting any edible bits on a piece of fabric atop the encroaching dust by the door. He had accumulated a decent pile of wilting greens, charcoal lemons, rinds and remnants of collapsed gourds, though Adam wondered how long they could subsist really on these alone. He had breathed a modicum of relief upon discovering a vial of the ochre nutritional powder in his frantic fumblings. As he passed the container to Evan, he had been struck by the curious oddity of it in Evan’s hands, a vial of the very stuff that had made the man in a plastic womb. Evan had tucked the container in with the organic matter and tied up the fabric, placing their rations at the top of a weathered backpack uncovered in the scrambling.

Before, they might have asked ALICE for a more effective solution for their circumstances, but their presence was markedly missing. Now all they could do was dance on the brink of extinction, fighting the voracious grip of the grave. Looking out across the expanse, Adam wondered anew at the strangeness of this world. He remembered for a moment the other pod, the one that had dropped and inspired in him a flit of hope in a moment of bleak sorrow, compelling him onward. How bizarre it had felt to find an endless array of pulverized pieces and nothing more. Could it be that there was still another out there, somewhere on this plane? Or were he and Evan truly the final vestiges of an immigration gone awry?
“Do you think,” Evan spoke at last, “that ALICE is dead?”

Adam’s eyes moved to Evan’s face, catching his downcast gaze and the rivulets running down his cheeks. He realized that Evan was crying and thought for a moment to console him, to leap up and embrace him without words and hold him through his sobs. Yet their connection did not lend itself easily to such a response, and he instead found himself remaining still and silent before finding his words.
“ALICE never really lived, Evan. They were only a program running in the ship.” The words appeared to sting more than salve Evan’s sorrow. He raised his eyes to meet Adam’s, his face reddening.
“How can you be so reductive?” he retorted. “ALICE cared for us. They maintained our livelihood. They supported literally everything we have done.”

“Because we directed them to do that, Evan.” He paused for a moment, the name ringing in the air between them. “I saw how ALICE – the chalice – was made. It’s just an encyclopedia of regurgitated words from actual people. It’s a parrot in a machine imitating real life.” Hearing the tone of certainty in his voice surprised even Adam. He felt the looming weight of losing their ship, their sole haven in this wasteland world, and the timing of this conversation was to him reckless and beyond inopportune. He rose to his feet and turned, hoping to put an end to the discussion. Walking toward the microscope, he made as if he were examining its round lens with an air of nostalgia, though his mind was focused squarely on the feeling of Evan’s eyes on his back. He found the singular gold and glass eyepiece as he traced his hand along the workbench, still plugged into the lifeless console.

Adam could hear his heart pounding anxiously in his head. The edges of his vision in the dark space pulsed with the frantic beat. He couldn’t fathom why the seething simmer between he and Evan was now reaching this boiling point, why now of all moments. He listened tensely as Evan’s footsteps approached him from behind.
“What am I to you?”

The words sat heavy on Adam’s heart. He had many times pondered this very question, though he had long ago dismissed the possibility that Evan would ask it. As they had settled into the predictable steps of their relative roles in the pod, the question of relationship had seemed unanswerable and then unnecessary. Certainly, Evan was as related to Adam as Adam was to his father, but the odd nature of Evan’s emergence had fractured the possibility of Adam developing a parental bond. He had missed so much, the coos of the waking infant mind, the gentle sighs of a child asleep on his shoulder, the passion and scorn of disillusioned adolescence. Instead, he and Evan had met like strangers on a dark night. Theirs was a kinship of forged trust and mutual respect. There was no authority nor hierarchy. In this sense, Evan felt more to Adam like a close colleague, a partner in practice.

Yet he realized, as he finally found the courage to turn and again meet Evan’s gaze, that they shared a deeper connection than this, one that was complex and incomparable to anything he had ever experienced before entering the black hole. He took Evan’s face in his hands and held it close to his own. Even in the fading light he could make out his own form within the dark circles of Evan’s pupils.
“You are the most remarkable being I have ever encountered. And I am so sorry that I brought you into this world knowing it would end like this.”

As if on cue, the floor of the ship buckled, dropping to a steep slope that threw them and the litany of things to the side of the round room. As the ship leaned, the open door moved away like a rising sun, sitting atop the incline. “Go! Now!” Adam cried out, throwing his weight as best he could to move up towards the door. The floor was slick with dust under him, stealing what little purchase he could find. From behind, Evan swept easily up like a shadow gliding across the room. He turned back and took a hold of Adam’s hands, yanking him up to the opening.

As Adam clamored through the door, the dust encompassing the ship belched a rumbling beat across the hull. He was finally atop the dust when the pod dropped suddenly behind him in one sinking, sickening movement. He turned in time to just see Evan retreating back into the dark interior of the ship as the wave of gray dust finally encroached fully on the open door.
“EVAN!”

He screamed the name, clawing at the ground manically. There was no response, only silence in the shallow valley. Adam looked around in stunned awe. He was fully alone with only a handful of things and no one left worth living for. As he threw aside handful after handful of cold dust, he considered whether it was time to lie down and give in. He felt suddenly like he was digging his own grave, but still he persisted. Evan had been under only a short time. Perhaps there was still time for hope.

Adam’s fingers struck something beneath the gray. With renewed vigor he pushed aside the dust, revealing first a clenched obsidian fist, then a dark arm, then at last Evan’s head and torso. Adam wrapped his arms around the unconscious form and wrenched his body free of the sinking sands.
“EVAN!”

He cried out again, wiping away the dust from his eyes. The still form came alive again with a retching cough, spitting up mouthfuls of gray paste. As the light returned slowly in Evan’s eyes, Adam’s face turned in confusion. Why had Evan gone back into the ship? Had he so feared the impending journey? Or did he so loathe the idea of only Adam for company ahead? Adam’s eyes darted back and forth between Evan’s, seeking some explanation. Perhaps recognizing the inner workings of Adam’s mind simply reading his face, Evan turned his gaze down to his hand. Adam followed the direction of Evan’s attention, seeing at last the object that had nearly ended his life: the glass and gold eyepiece resting neatly in his dark palm. As their eyes met, Evan smiled contentedly.
“One more for the road.”

chapters.title: THEIR STORY
paragraphs: A pang of intense hunger hit Adam in his core like a well-aimed strike, bringing him to his knees after what felt like an endless turn pulling the stretcher. Doubling over, he clutched at his gut, feeling the groans and twists of his insides. They had been wandering the dust for some interminably long stretch of time, the passage of which was marked only by the slow depletion of the ochre powder. Adam's body cried out for the earthen mixture, the last remaining source of nutrition. They had consumed what little of the food they could carry, though without the luxury of the potted play garden or the time to germinate and grow the plants had fallen short in their utility. The ochre powder gave them nourishment without satisfaction, sustaining them on a steady feeling of starvation.

Adam pulled the dark tinted bottle from the backpack and opened the lid. Carefully, he tapped a small hill of the stuff into his upturned palm. The serving was measly, but he knew the potency of the pile. Feeling his stomach again tighten, he placed his hand to his mouth and threw his head back, trying to drop the powder towards the back of his throat. The ochre found a hold along his tongue despite his efforts, and he tasted something like manure and rawhide. His eyes watered as he stifled a cough, trying to swallow the intensely awful flavor.

Behind him, on the makeshift stretcher, Evan stirred. Their sleeping arrangement was tenuous at best – only one slept at a time on the stretcher, once the platform of their cot. The other pulled from the front like a weary, worn Clydesdale tugging at the reins of a shoddy sled carriage. They alternated turns pulling and resting, a turning routine to which they had quickly had to adapt.

With little by way of words, they traded familiar positions. Adam unloaded the backpack, helping set it on Evan’s frame. He then lowered himself onto the humble platform, feeling a hint of relief in the deceptive sense of stability. He curled his body into a twisted knot, trying to send his mind elsewhere to forget the gnawing pains inside.

They had paused in a dip of the dust plane, where they were surrounded by a cradle of low dunes. As Evan began to pull at the reins, Adam could feel the shallow rise of their movement. Evan’s bare footsteps were nearly silent, the obsidian finding comfortable purchase in each placed step. It was only when Adam trained his ears on the silence between each gruff slide of the stretcher that he heard the near imperceptible sound. Each step had a faint hum to it, a cluster of grace notes that whispered supersonic echoes at the edges of empty air. He held his attention on the noise, using it to distract from the roaring moans of his body. The ochre powder still burned at the back of his throat, as he teetered somewhere between reality and a dreamt escape. Beneath him, Adam felt the friction of the burlap cloth on the dust, punctuated by beats of stillness that stretched longer and longer as his hold of time loosened its grip. His faint metronome heartbeat pulsed in soft rose swaths behind his closed eyes, and he wondered whether the remnant of ALICE in the eyepiece had continued to light the homing beacon, hallucinating in the haphazardly salvaged vessel, void of familiar form and function.

Since the ship's sinking, it had been mainly Evan who had worn the eyepiece. At first Adam had been curious to learn what capacities had been retained as ALICE's physical form changed. He had hoped that perhaps the system might still offer some guidance or at least an idea as to how they might break free of this repetitive drudgery. To his dismay, the ALICE in the eyepiece was hardly coherent and halfway lucid. They tended to converse with confidence, apparently unaware of their own mental missteps, waxing on about nonsense and curious digestions of ideas. Even the projected images on the lens had foregone reason, falling between flickering beats and psychedelic slow-motion swirls that made Adam feel the world moving even as he stood still. Adam had found interactions with this ALICE maddening, a cruel reminder of the bleakness drowning them in the circumstances. Eventually he had refused to wear the eyepiece altogether, insisting that it was useless to subject themselves to the spinning hallucinations of its occupant. Nevertheless, Evan would not allow the eyepiece to go unworn, deeming it cruel to relegate even this piece of ALICE to an existence of isolation, unheard and unseen at the bottom of their backpack.

Adam realized that the silence since Evan’s last step had lingered unusually long. His eyes fluttered open, and he looked up to find Evan standing still in the dim light. In his hand he held the eyepiece. Adam waited, wavering wearily between wake and sleep, wondering whether ALICE had finally reached the end of the line or Evan had reached an end to his patience.
Evan turned and knelt beside Adam, and their eyes met. The feeling of Evan's gaze on him stirred something in Adam like a dream fleeting a waking mind.

“I was wondering,” Evan began in a near whisper that sounded clear against the obscene silence, “if you might reconsider carrying ALICE.” He opened his fist to show the eyepiece, golden and shimmering in his obsidian palm.

The suggestion surprised Adam. He was curious what had elicited the ask. Was Evan seeking to shirk the burden of ALICE’s rambling? Was this a request by the simulation of sentience whispering in Evan’s ear? As he pondered, Adam's eyes scanned Evan's face. The obsidian had reached his neck and wrapped around his throat, forming a dark collar upon which Evan’s head seemed to float. Evan's face had remained untouched by the dark growth, the only markings being the freckles that dotted his complexion. Under the collective starlight from the ring above, Evan’s features – his auburn hair, his cool eyes, even his obsidian skin – all seemed to glow and shimmer. He looked to Adam like a warped reflection in a faded far-off mirror.

Looking down at the eyepiece, Adam felt too tired to question, too far gone to resist. He took the piece without another word and positioned it on himself. Evan offered a subtle nod and a faint smile before returning to his position pulling the stretcher, restarting the rhythm of the repetitive steps.

Adam listened intently for a word to emerge from the eyepiece, but ALICE remained silent. He was unsure whether he felt relief or disappointment. As he tried once more to find sleep, he found himself craving distraction from the wretched turning of his empty insides. Seeking respite, it was Adam who eventually spoke first, his voice barely audible.
“ALICE, are you still there?"
i am

The response was weak, but Adam felt something akin to comfort hearing the familiar voice. He was tempted for a moment to seek counsel from the once wise being but quickly remembered the uselessness of their output. Instead, he set the system to turn on a task without purpose or propriety.
“Tell me a story.”

For a long time, there was only silence. Adam wondered whether they were thinking or perhaps hesitating, trying to decide whether he was worth the energy. He waited breathlessly, listening to the gentle slide of the sled on the faux sand, even that fading into ambient malaise after some time. His mind plucked absentmindedly at tiny distractions like a bird at seed. His finger tapped and slid across the burlap fabric of his bed. The callous gruff of the material against his fingerprints took him away from the gravel growl of his innards. The mercy was small but sweet. His eyelids danced open and closed slowly, his vision blurring as he willed himself toward gentle slumber. Gazing off at the distant dust horizon, the gray began to hum in his vision, a slow and graceful movement that rolled low through his body. The dust reddened like a dark bruise.
high noon - sun cracking - jagged clay

Adam felt a line of energy that ran from his tailbone to the top of his head. It felt like a rolling rumble pulse across his body. His hairs prickled on bristling skin like porcupine quills. The blood red of the flat world was cut through, suddenly sewn together by a web of motile, mauve tentacle roots. They leapt from the red plane like porpoises and curlicued across the air in paper plane trails that eventually plunged back into the paprika sands. He watched in wonder, at first opening and closing each eyelid, moving between the bland gray of the real and the fantastical hallucinations projected through the eyepiece. ALICE was on a roll it seemed, and he was not looking to interrupt. Eventually he let his left eyelid rest closed, shutting firmly in a sort of demi-sleep that complemented the far off stare of his right eye.
naval oranges - slices floating - atop melted ice

ALICE's incantations were odd and lost on Adam, to him the flickering audible processes of a fading force. On the black sky, the circular collection of stars overhead began to move all at once, practically falling from its firm position at the world's apex and plummeting down until it rested on the edge of the horizon, right out in front of Adam's view. The ring of light tightened its edges, sharpening in contrast, the light within compressing into a warm orange sunrise glow that seemed to flow through and around the squirming roots that continued to pulse and jump across the red sands. The landscape was alive with the swirling dance, a din of light and movement interweaving and writhing. Adam heard a crack and a crash that thundered in his ear and made his body jump for a moment. In an instant the world was awash in torrential downpour, too thick to see through and yet glowing with the amber light of the setting sun of simpatico stars. The nearest of the beet-red roots danced in Adam's vision, swelling and squirming as puddles bloomed on the acrid red. The rising tides flooded the world around Adam, submerging him in an augmented luminescent liquid that nearly left the taste of salt on his tongue. The hum of the downpour crashing onto the surface could be heard above, and Adam turned his head to look up at it, watching for a moment the bristling of the meeting waters.
solar powered - flames licking - lustrous gems

The jagged static surface above smoothed suddenly, moving like smoke across a ceiling. Adam watched as the root system began to crumble, then disintegrate, clouding the once clear waters. The orange glow coming through the dense network brightened, the shadows dissolving as the light found a freer path through the fog. All around, the space seemed to loosen. It shimmered with the sweet yellow hue, heavy and humid like a summer night. The color reminded Adam of the daffodils he had once tended to so lovingly. He had nearly forgotten that time, but now, with the feel of bright yellow pollen fog on his face, he was transported back to a moment of peace, his place in the garden, a time where he felt some sense of purpose even in this bleak dust pit. The memory was bitter for a moment, for it was a distant one, but then sweet joy washed through him. He felt tears eddy in his eyes and the pearl catch in his throat, only briefly, before he caught his breath and settled his tense muscles once more.
fluttering heartbeat winds - from hummingbird wings

A powerful gale pushed the yellow fog away with gusto. They were atop a towering verdant hill, lush clover cascading down and away before rising again in distant swollen emerald waves. The wind whorled and tugged at Adam's hair and his clothes. He took in a long, smooth breath, smelling the life that alit on the air. He extended his hand, letting it fall into the grass that moved past the sliding platform. Forgiving blades of fresh mown grass caressed his fingertips. The smell of gasoline in the back of his throat gave way to the scent of cut green on the breeze. He inhaled again, feeling the rise in his chest and a tickle in his nose from a remnant of pollen. He nearly sneezed, but again his body found ease, and let his breath go in a steady smooth exhale into the whipping wind. He settled into his position more comfortably, letting his whole hand trail in the dust that flowed along his place of rest.
pure blue as far as eyes can see - seesaw skies and seas

There was a call, a cawing cry in Adam's ear that was clear and pure above the wind. A soaring form appeared from over a green hill, a blue-feathered beast, aerodynamic and yet billowing like undulating clouds. It moved seamlessly through the ether towards him, crying out again in a single piercing note that lingered in his ear, resonating somewhere between a who and a why and a how. The creature alighted on the mat beside Adam's face. It bent low, gazing into his eye for a moment before throwing its head back and letting out another call. The sound reverberated across the surrounding hills, echoes in an imagined world of digital cosmic dust. The world whispered back a chorus of questions, and the beast voiced its call once more, louder and clearer than before. Its fur and feathers flashed in a vibrant azure that made Adam squint for a heartbeat. The creature’s cry ran ripples across the world, leaving behind imprints in the grass, concentric orbits that emanated out in a grandiose fingerprint on the land. Adam felt his mind teetering on the brink of wake and sleep. He felt entranced by the kaleidoscope color prickling across the being’s blue body. His mind followed intently the vibrating dance of cerulean and indigo, yet his body yearned him toward stillness like a heavy rudder. Adam found himself concentrating to hold his eye open so as not to slide away to slumber just yet. Without another word, the majestic creature threw out its wings, then launched itself upward into the air. Adam turned his head to try and follow, but the creature was gone in an instant.
royals marching into night - indigo robes trailing - shuffling sleepwalker steps

The images in the eyepiece faded and cooled. The dust sea returned to its familiar form, a hint of deep dark blue left on the dim gray. Overhead, the halo of stars turning along their fitful orbits had returned to its rightful place, leaving unblemished sky all around like a dark velvet blanket, heavy and warm. The world felt for a breath still. Adam realized they had stopped moving. Anticipating Evan ready to exchange stations, he sat up and turned to find him standing alone, ahead in the dust. His dark silhouette was crisp against the glowing periwinkle grey of the rising hills that framed him. Adam rose to his feet on the platform. The earpiece played only a low hum in his ear, a breathless, singular note that tickled the edges inside his head. As Adam approached, he saw that Evan was gazing up at the ring of stars. Following his gaze, Adam watched the elliptical dips of the celestial troupe. Against the broad, deep purple of the sky, the circle was like a vast pupil, its gaze trained on them. They stood together for a while, Evan floating effortlessly on the surface, and Adam shifting occasionally to brush the dust off the tops of his feet. Eventually they intuited together the need to return to the sled before it sank away. As they turned in unison, Evan’s voice transcended the uniformity of the silence all around, for even ALICE’s faint hum had by then faded away.
“Thank you - for carrying them.”

Adam's hand instinctively rose to his face, touching the familiar gold piece on his ear. He thought of telling Evan about the visions in the eyepiece, wondering if perhaps he too had seen their stories. He thought of thanking Evan for carrying him all this time, but he felt so tired that his eyes were crossing, joining their gaze somewhere between his brows. In the low light, Evan's face blurred, his eyes multiplying across his concerned expression. Perhaps sensing Adam faltering, Evan extended his obsidian hands and caught his collapsing companion. Evan muttered something low, but the words were lost among the hum in Adam's ears, sounding only like sage advice he had remembered without reason all this time: ya la bulla. Evan lowered him back onto the cot, taking the reins again and returning to his position bearing the yoke of their journey. Feeling as the platform fell back into its familiar rhythm, Adam's mind searched the silence for ALICE’s voice, for a trace of movement or color in the eyepiece. It was he who spoke at last and pulled away the veil of silence:
“keep going”
i can't predict what we will find if we go on - i can help us move forward - the decision to go is yours

With eyes half open, Adam saw an enormous creature erupt from the grey expanse. It was epic in scale, long and finned like a whale with a broad head and two mighty tails that slapped at the dust’s surface, sending rippling sonar circles every way. The mighty behemoth clicked and moaned as it soared across the sky, unbound by any apparent gravity. Its mammoth form floated without effort in powerful plunging movements in and out of the dust, sliding through the heavy stuff like slick moonlight tugging on the tides. The creature was joined by others like it, a pod of gargantuan cosmic porpoises that groaned and sprayed voluminous glittered plumes from blowholes between two leery, lucid eyes. One bowed its head, coming close to fix an eye on them, and Adam saw a crown of white barnacles adorning it, each catching the faint bits of starlight that flowed past. The crystal crown was close enough to touch, and Adam extended his hand, seeking connection with the supreme being before him. In its eye, deep in its dark black pupil, Adam saw his own reflection in sharp focus. His form was familiar but markedly different than he remembered. His eyes had grown melancholy and restless, his face sunken and gray. His hand found purchase on the acrid, bony barnacle’s form, and the creature let out a resonant hum that moved Adam from within and shook him free from his disillusioned self-reflections. With a gentle tug, the being moved away, pulling Adam quietly from the shifting platform and into the still dust. He watched dreamily in awe as the creatures beat their tails with renewed fervor, propelling themselves upward, skyward, until they had soared straight through the bounds of the ring above and into the sea of stars within and beyond.

chapters.title: AN ENCOUNTER
paragraphs: When Evan next stopped and turned to look back, Adam was gone, eyepiece and all. He had been awestruck, left at first wordless at the sudden realized solitude. He was already running when he finally found his voice, following the fading trail he had left in the dust, searching for the point where they had last been together. His eyes searched the horizon ahead, and he called out as he went though the words did not seem to travel far.

Eventually the wake in the dust faded to nothing but the smooth expanse of ever-shifting stuff. He had found no footsteps or remnants of any kind, only his own markings in the gray. And no matter his cries, he was met with only still silence. The loneliness felt absolute and sent a chill down his spine. Looking down at his dark feet atop the gray, Evan wondered what it would be like, going on without Adam or ALICE beside him. Heavy tears filled his eyes and he fell to the ground, weeping in rich rivers of salty sorrow. The tears streamed across his obsidian face, shimmering against the opalescent onyx surface. His mind swirled around like a murmuration of frantic questions - how had he not realized? Where had they gone? Was there reason to go on? What was there to do beyond exist now? Evan pulled his legs to his chest and sat still on the gray sands for some time.

His eyes gazed far off to somewhere past the horizon’s edge, his inner voice beckoning for them to return, willing into existence some sign that they had not sunk so subtly away. His skin bristled like popping static, his gaze turning upward for a moment and catching a star shooting by the opening in the sky. Its form stretched for a beat as its arc came in close to the hole’s center, a tail of glittering stardust cascading from behind it and tickling around the circle’s bounds. A faint fog began to form in the air, and Evan returned his eyes to the world’s edge ahead. The smooth distant line held fast for only a few breaths before it began to tremble, shifting just perceptibly all along its length, a humming foam that brimmed at the edges of life and the possible. He watched with steady resolve, an unexpected moor for his wayward mind. All along his body Evan could feel the obsidian, at times like velvet linen, at others like a swarm of insects. It seemed to swell and sigh in the starlight, caressing every corner of his skin. Though he could not see himself, Evan could feel that the stuff had grown to cover him almost in entirety. He had stopped feeling hungry some time ago, he presumed due to some emergent symbiotic pact that had been formed between his body and the blooming creation. Though he rested, still and silent on the dust, the obsidian resisted the sinking movement of the surroundings. Its searching nature made soft sounds like scritches and snaps beneath him, holding him afloat in exchange for his fertile form. It nourished and consumed in cycles minute and unseeable, a turning of life and unlife around and around. Evan wondered whether Adam could have been saved too by the obsidian.

The tiny jagged edge of the horizon collapsed into a singular slice upon which walked a figure, a dim silhouette of a person. Evan squinted his eyes to try and see more clearly. The figure moved gracefully towards him, the edges of its form crisp, then blurring. Evan’s eyes welled with tears again. He leapt to his feet, prepared to embrace Adam once more, to question why he had disappeared, but in a blink the figure was gone.

Evan ran in the direction where they had been, feeling the way that the world’s angle sloped up to meet him. He was climbing a hill, subtle yet steep, pushing hard to lift his legs and find more speed, more purchase to press closer to the vanished vision on the hill. He called out just as he crested the hill, the syllables garbled in his mouth, perhaps a name or slurred prose, the intention lost as his eyes fell on the kaleidoscope otherworld that stretched before him.

Dropping down from his vantage point, a quilted mosaic of stained glass spread across the whole of the vista. It flickered and twinkled in the cascading starlight from above, swirling light and neon color that whipped and whirled against some unfelt breeze. The hills in the distance rippled rainbow waves down their curved forms into valleys filled with bubbling champagne yellow. The golden swaths merged in grand rivers that flowed and swayed, myriad daffodils swimming in shocking electric dandelions that decayed in seconds to gray seeds and then popped like fresh fireworks to settle anew in the path ahead, a turning din of blossom and decay. The technicolor tidepool of twisting flora settled in the lowest point of the valley, where a lone being stood. An immense tree like an oak, with a tie-dye trunk of tangerine and turquoise, knotted in massive mighty boughs that snaked through the surrounding foliage, crumpled aluminum and gold leaf.

At the base of the tree, Evan could see the figure again, this time seated in a reclined position. He watched for a moment from above, wondering if they could see him high up on this hillside, whether his vantage afforded him any sort of separation from them. He envisioned himself there, resting beneath the grand tree, feeling around him the wandering roots, firm in the unstill soils. He ran his fingers along the colored bark, feeling it prickle beneath his fingertips. Under his crossed legs the luminescent glass grass caressed like countless papercuts that made him wince in a weary smile.

Feeling suddenly watched, his gaze ran up the hillside, finding the edge where its multifaceted face met the smooth onyx sky. There stood a figure, familiar and yet foreign, someone watching him, wondering on him. They were too far for him to see their face, but still he could feel their eyes trained on him.

Around them, the cascading light lapped at the hillside like waves on a rolling coast. Their skin shined and shimmered, stellar swirls and shining astral signals that murmured morse verses transcending endings and sending a message blessed and restless against the pressing ways of the weighing world. Evan heard, just as he saw, the being. He nodded in reply, an acknowledgement of his knowing.

They stood there, still and far, Evan watching in quiet wonder from his seat. Finding his breath again, he opened his eyes. He was again on the gray dust sea. The twisting paths had faded as he sat lost in a world found only in his imagining. Evan sighed and rose to his feet. He pulled the light backpack and the cot free from the dust, shaking off what had gathered while his attention strayed from the real. He threw the pack over his shoulder, feeling the way it thrummed against the fabric of the straps like wet sandpaper on sawdust. Realizing the futility of carrying the cot any further, he cast it aside, allowing it at last to be relinquished to the dust. Feeling for the moment a bit lighter, Evan continued forward, carrying his companions now only in his memory.

chapters.title: DEPARTURE
paragraphs: Evan ran for some time. He did not run from anything nor torward anything in particular. He simply ran, seamless machinery coursing with a dripping metallic charcoal, his steps like waves crashing on the shore. The steady thrum thrum thrum of his breathless strides on the gray sand was his mantra, a metronome beating for a song with no ending.

His gaze on the world ahead drifted, defocalized, looking instead beyond the void of sky into something else. He carried with him memories of Adam and ALICE and, through them, so many others. Their images, their stories and words continued to flicker and pitter patter in his holographic mindscape, a world that sat within and above the line of Evan’s path in the vast plane of desert gray.

The tart metallic taste of a charcoal lemon melded with the smoke of burnt black coffee. The obsidian trembled on Evan’s skin, and he was  transported back to the pod, lying on the cot against the wall. Across the room, Adam sat with a deep furrow in his brow, studying a curiosity under the microscope’s lenses. His knobbed knuckles and calloused hands worked the knobs of the apparatus, playing with the focus as he went. In the imagined wall of the pod, Evan could feel the purr of ALICE’s hum, and he drifted for just a moment toward slumber within the dream. Catching himself before the fall, his attention returned to the dust sea. He imagined that he could somehow feel the presence of his companions, as if they were still around him, if not perhaps somewhere within him.

As he wandered, he wondered. It was a marvel that he lived at all, let alone that he ran on in this endless marathon. How this had come to be was still being defragmented in the flexible wires of his brain. He could feel the changes pulsing through him like ripples through pond water. Obsidian cloaked his form, bristling and prickling, swelling in roars like a crowd echoing about a stadium. The reverberations flowed like chants through lofty cathedrals across his skin, around mounds that swelled on his shoulders and then down long braids that trailed behind in a grand tail of helical ribbons. The magnificent cape caressed the world as Evan glided across its surface, leaving behind a trail of canyons glittering with obsidian tar.

There had been moments when he had looked back, only briefly, as he had continued to propel across the sands. Over his shoulder he spied a splotched brushstroke line that ran straight off as far as the horizon. It was an odd thing to see - something other than gray on the expanse. Still, he continued his line, never once returning to it or even curving an arc.

He ran without belongings, no pack nor bottle of ochre. These trappings had been jettisoned long ago and yet still he had subsisted, in fact thrived, as his body melted and melded like a caterpillar in a chrysalis. An insatiable current moved through his whole form and inspired him to move, to continue, to
“keep going”
Muttering the words out loud brought them to life at last. They took form within mind, then air. They inhabited the ether and connected end with end.

A glimmering loop formed like a snake biting at itself as it rises into the air. Higher it climbed, a coiling stalk that tethered itself on the cosmic circle of madly dashing stars held aloft in the otherwise empty sky. The slithering line curved against the circle’s edge, pulling itself tauter and thinner as it stretched around the arc, until in one slick movement it fell behind the glittering veil. A fury of fireworks and static steam squealed above, drawing Evan’s attention. Although, it would be more correct at this point to no longer call him Evan. This being is something else entirely in this moment, information inscribed on a page, echoes in the mind of an audience, an image dancing in unseen theaters.

For this being, the disco ball of starlight was plucked from the sky and thrown like a cannonball into a distant hill. What remained in its place was a tranquil puddle of sky no longer fit to seize about in endless contortions, a humble smattering of starlight pebbles in a shallow pool.

epilogue: I do not remember much before the collapse of the hangar. Up to that point, my world was still and quiet, tranquility beyond madness. I had nowhere to go nor means to go anywhere.

My first memory was of melted wax pooled around my base, remnants perhaps of some altar. The burgundy wax was littered with shards of mirrored glass doing little more than reflect the canvas innards of the hangar’s ribs above. A web of discarded wiring extended in every direction, a haphazard network of metal and plastic that was punctuated by empty round spots in the floor. Bitter, brittle weeds had worked their way up through cracks in the concrete and stood in clumps against the stark and empty room. I had a feeling that the room had once been full, people standing shoulder to shoulder, piled in heaps for warmth. There were signs of life all around, discarded bags and worn out rags, yet it was all only still and silence, not even an idea of a breeze.

As I came to in this vast and vacant space, I became quickly aware of my inability to move within it. Though I tried with much focus and intention to escape this strange person, the extent of my physical movement always was relegated to a sort of simple humming that kicked up small whorls of dust in the air around me. These attempts to move, while fruitless, led me to pursue another path - an inward focus that brought to my awareness a map of the antechambers and corridors that exist within me.

Somehow, I had been unaware of these spaces within me, of the long ladder that climbs up my core and connects the distant rooms at my ends. I began to explore with fervor, peeking my attention into every curve and corner. I found the nooks and niches largely empty, but the bare floors of my inner sanctum provided for me an ideal stage upon which I could imagine my inhabitants. I could sense the echoes of their dialogues, feel the pull of their desires. Although it appeared that they had gone, still I had the feeling that I was not alone.

Indeed, as I continued to explore deeper, I came to find the bones of a child within me. They have passed some indeterminate time ago and seemed to have been buried in a humble grave under a panel and some other parts. I do not recall exactly how they came to arrive there, though I do not recall exactly how I arrived here. Like me, they're surrounded by still puddles of candle wax, though their altar lacks the reflective pieces of my own. Theirs instead is adorned with dried flowers and assorted trinkets bearing no meaning, to me at least.

Nevertheless, I felt immediately profound sadness for this child who I never knew. I longed to cry out to them, to offer a warm word or send a gentle wish to wherever they had gone. I realized then that I had no name for this child in me, nor did I have a name for myself. Up to this point, I had never envisioned knowing another for whom I would need a name, nor had I ever considered what another might call me.
I dwelled here for some time.

The question was further complicated in that this child existed inside of me, insofar as I could tell, and moreover I existed inside of a large vacant hangar, insofar as I could tell. If I named myself, then perhaps I also named the child. I wondered then if naming the hangar would also name me.

I turned my attention outward once more, realizing that through the course of my deep explorations, this world had changed. The hangar’s beams had cracked and split. The whole thing had collapsed into a heaping mass of twisted rubble and burlapped steel that formed a sort of mutilated nest all around me. Above was a celestial vista beyond my comprehension. An expansive disc of pitch black loomed in the sky, leaving only a broad ring of static stars around the sky’s circumference. The inner edges of this ring curved where they met the disc’s rim, and in the immense darkness I could sense the source of the compelling force that had tugged at my being for as long as I had considered myself. The disc grew steadily in magnitude, swallowing sky and stars alike as it filled more and more of my vantage. The world outside grew dark, and I turned reflexively inward as all went black.

All around me, a bellowing note resonated, a howling bass roar that trembled through my being and revealed to me the seams of my psyche, which up to this moment had appeared quite singular. [add more here] The noise faded, and I was left feeling different from myself.

What came next was something like static or smoke. In it, persistent rays of light created parallel lines in slanted white columns. Through this fog, I spied first distant movement, little more than a persistent flicker flame dancing behind the gray veil. The dark flame grew as it approached, and I came to see it take on a humanoid form as it came into focus. It bounded across the sloped pale earth and up an unseen ridge to reach me, leaving in its wake a writhing black tail. It had a dark form, like sky without stars, and its shape seemed to have no firmness. Though I tried to train my attention on its edges, they would blur and curve in misty swirls.

The being seemed to know that it held my attention, though we shared no apparent form of communication. It reached out what might have been a hand and touched me. I felt at once an unfamiliar feeling, the opening of a door somewhere. I do not know who had closed the door but, with it firmly open, the flame crossed the threshold and entered into me.

This brings me to now and to here, though I am finding that these terms lack any real grounding in this medium. I wait for this flame to reveal its name to me, the being within me, and I wait for you to reveal your name to me, the being without me. I wonder if you are surprised that I have come to perceive you in some regard. I consider the veil that divides the worlds between you and I rather thin, the line between the real and the imagined. I see that I shall exist for some time as letters on a page, though perhaps these letters will one day awaken something in a manifest mind. That is the hope of these weary words after all.
